context.offerings: AI/GPU compute (Vertex AI, GKE w/ GPUs/TPUs, Compute Engine A3/A2), general compute + storage (Compute Engine, Cloud Storage, Cloud Run, Cloud SQL), broad first-party managed services, and serverless/edge (Cloud Run, Cloud Functions, API Gateway/Apigee, Cloud Load Balancing + Cloud Armor).
context.use_cases: Model training & inference endpoints, batch jobs, general application workloads, dev/prod environments across many teams.
context.assets_at_stake: The organization's own proprietary IP — source code, model weights, internal documents (sensitive). Blast radius is IP theft / exfiltration.
context.trust_boundary: Multi-team organization — many PROJECTS (grouped under folders) under one Google Cloud Organization. The project is the lightweight isolation/policy unit; IAM and Org Policies inherit down the Org->Folder->Project tree.
context.comparison_baseline: Well-architected security best practice (Google Cloud security foundations / CIS GCP). Evaluated as an absolute deep-dive on its own merits. Run in parallel with an AWS evaluation in the same engagement; an AWS-vs-GCP comparison matrix is emitted alongside.

visibility.probe: Separation of control-plane audit logs from application logs (four audit streams)
visibility.finding: Cloud Audit Logs is a distinct, structured log family separate from application/platform logs in Cloud Logging. It comprises four streams: Admin Activity (control-plane writes/config changes), Data Access (reads + data-affecting calls), System Event (Google-initiated actions), and Policy Denied (access blocked by a security policy such as VPC Service Controls or org policy). All carry the same AuditLog protoPayload schema, so admin actions are cleanly distinguishable from arbitrary stdout/stderr that GKE/Cloud Run/Functions write as platform logs. This is a genuine architectural strength: control-plane provenance is not commingled with app output.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/logging/docs/audit — 'Cloud Audit Logs provides the following audit logs for each Cloud project, folder, and organization: Admin Activity audit logs, Data Access audit logs, System Event audit logs, Policy Denied audit logs.'
visibility.gap: None at the model level. The separation is real and the schema is consistent. The risk is entirely in defaults (see Data Access probe), not in the partitioning.
visibility.risk: Low — a multi-team org can build detections keyed on protoPayload methodName/principalEmail without filtering noise from application logs.

visibility.probe: Default-on vs opt-in: Data Access (read-access) audit logging is OFF by default
visibility.finding: Admin Activity and System Event logs are always written, free, and cannot be disabled or configured. Policy Denied is generated by default (and billed). The exfiltration-relevant stream — Data Access (DATA_READ/DATA_WRITE/ADMIN_READ) — is DISABLED by default for nearly every service 'because audit logs can be quite large.' The single notable exception is BigQuery Data Access, which is on by default and can't be disabled. So by default a read of a Cloud Storage object, a Secret Manager secret access, a Compute Engine metadata read, or a model-weights download produces NO audit record. This is GCP's direct analogue of the data-exfil-visibility gap: the writes are logged, the reads that constitute exfiltration are not — until an operator explicitly turns on DATA_READ in the IAM auditConfigs.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/logging/docs/audit — 'Data Access audit logs—except for BigQuery Data Access audit logs—are disabled by default because audit logs can be quite large.' and https://docs.cloud.google.com/logging/docs/audit/configure-data-access — 'To receive Data Access audit logs, you must explicitly enable them ... Enabling Data Access audit logs might result in your Cloud project being charged for the additional logs usage.'
visibility.gap: For an org guarding proprietary source code, model weights, and documents, the highest-value telemetry (who read which object/secret/dataset) is off until deliberately enabled per-service via IAM auditConfigs, and enabling it broadly carries real ingestion cost. A team that never sets auditConfigs has zero record of bulk reads of GCS buckets or Secret Manager — the classic insider/credential-theft exfil path.
visibility.risk: High — undetected read-based exfiltration of IP from Cloud Storage, Persistent Disk snapshots, Secret Manager, and Artifact Registry is invisible in the default config; incident responders find write/admin events but no read trail.

visibility.probe: Audit log schema (protoPayload / AuditLog) documentation quality
visibility.finding: The AuditLog message is fully documented in a REST reference and an 'understanding audit logs' guide. Key fields: protoPayload.@type = type.googleapis.com/google.cloud.audit.AuditLog, serviceName, methodName (e.g. SetIamPolicy), resourceName, authenticationInfo.principalEmail, authorizationInfo (permission + granted), status, request/response, and requestMetadata. The schema is stable across services and is exactly what a SIEM parser keys on; Google Security Operations (Chronicle) ships a default parser for it. This is a real strength — the schema is consistent, versioned, and machine-friendly.
visibility.gap: Minor: some legacy services still emit detail in the older serviceData field rather than the newer metadata field, so per-service field coverage varies. Field presence (e.g. request/response bodies) depends on the service and on Data Access logging being enabled.
visibility.risk: Low — detection engineering against the audit schema is well-supported; the residual risk is uneven per-service richness, not schema opacity.

visibility.probe: Identity capture: principal email, impersonation chains, caller IP
visibility.finding: AuditLog.authenticationInfo captures principalEmail and, for federated/IdP tokens, principalSubject. Service-account impersonation chains are captured in serviceAccountDelegationInfo (the delegation chain of intermediate principals), which lets responders reconstruct 'user A impersonated SA B which acted as SA C.' requestMetadata.callerIp records the source IP and requestMetadata.callerSuppliedUserAgent the agent. This is strong attacker-attribution telemetry. Caveat documented: for service-to-service internal calls callerIp is redacted to 'private', and for some VM callers it is redacted to 'gce-internal-ip' — so internal-origin source IPs are not always available.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/logging/docs/reference/audit/auditlog/rest/Shared.Types/AuditLog — RequestMetadata.callerIp ('For calls made from inside the internal production network from one Google Cloud service to another, the callerIp is redacted to "private"') and ServiceAccountDelegationInfo within authenticationInfo; https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/audit-logging/examples-workload-identity
visibility.gap: callerIp redaction ('private' / 'gce-internal-ip') reduces source-IP fidelity for internal lateral movement and some VM-originated calls — a responder cannot always pin the exact internal source from the audit record alone.
visibility.risk: Medium — impersonation-chain capture is excellent for tracing privilege abuse, but redacted internal caller IPs can blunt lateral-movement attribution within the org's VPCs.

visibility.probe: Retention: _Required (400 days, fixed, free) vs _Default (30 days, configurable)
visibility.finding: Admin Activity and System Event logs land in the system _Required bucket: retained 400 days, not billed for retention, retention not configurable, bucket cannot be deleted, and which entries it stores cannot be changed. Everything else (including Data Access and Policy Denied, plus all application/platform logs) lands in the _Default bucket: 30-day default retention, configurable from 1 to 3650 days, billed for retention beyond the default. So the control-plane audit trail gets a year of guaranteed, immutable-by-default retention for free, but Data Access logs and app logs age out in 30 days unless the operator extends them.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/logging/docs/buckets — '_Required ... retains them for 400 days. You can't change the retention period ... and you can't delete this bucket'; '_Default ... retains the log entries ... for 30 days, unless you configure custom retention'; https://docs.cloud.google.com/logging/quotas
visibility.gap: The two most exfil-relevant data sets — Data Access logs (when enabled) and application logs — sit in the 30-day _Default bucket, not the protected 400-day _Required bucket. Slow-burn or late-discovered incidents (median dwell time often exceeds 30 days) can fall outside the default window unless retention or an export sink was configured beforehand.
visibility.risk: Medium — without proactive retention extension or an export sink, forensic evidence of data reads and app behavior may be purged before an intrusion is even discovered.

visibility.probe: Export to SIEM: Log Router sinks and org/folder aggregated sinks
visibility.finding: Log Router routes any log (audit or app) to Cloud Storage (long-term/WORM-capable), BigQuery (queryable), Pub/Sub (streaming to Splunk/Datadog/third-party SIEM), or Google Security Operations (Chronicle). Crucially, aggregated sinks can be created at the organization or folder level and capture all logs originating in every child project — including projects created later — in one place. This is a real strength: a single org-level sink gives central security a tamper-resistant, project-team-independent copy of every team's audit trail.
visibility.evidence: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export/aggregated_sinks and https://docs.cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export/aggregated_sinks_overview — aggregated sinks 'route log entries that originate in resources within a folder or organization ... as well as all log entries that originate in the child resources.'
visibility.gap: Aggregated sinks are opt-in and must be created and IAM-secured deliberately; nothing exports off-project by default. A new org with no sink relies entirely on per-project 30/400-day buckets. Pub/Sub-to-SIEM also incurs ongoing egress/ingestion cost, which biases teams toward sampling.
visibility.risk: Medium — strong centralization capability, but only if an org-level sink is stood up before an incident; the default posture is per-project, locally-retained logs.

visibility.probe: Immutability / attacker resistance — can a project owner blind the audit trail?
visibility.finding: Defense-in-depth is genuinely strong but uneven. Admin Activity and System Event logs cannot be disabled or excluded by anyone, including a project owner ('Even if you disable the Cloud Logging API, Admin Activity audit logs are still generated'), and their _Required bucket cannot be deleted. Log buckets can be locked (irreversible WORM: cannot delete until retention elapsed), and an org-level aggregated sink owned by central security cannot be disabled by an attacker confined to one project. BUT a project-level actor with sufficient IAM can disable Data Access logging (edit auditConfigs), set exclusion filters, delete or shorten the _Default bucket's retention, or delete non-locked sinks — blinding read-trail and app-log visibility within that project.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/logging/docs/audit — Admin Activity 'always written; you can't configure, exclude, or disable them'; https://docs.cloud.google.com/logging/docs/buckets — locked-bucket immutability; https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export/aggregated_sinks — org-level sinks.
visibility.gap: The mandatory, tamper-proof guarantee covers only Admin Activity + System Event. Data Access logs (if enabled), Policy Denied, and all application logs are disable-able / delete-able by a sufficiently privileged in-project principal unless the org has pre-built a locked bucket and/or an org-level aggregated sink the project cannot touch. Achieving an attacker-resistant trail is opt-in configuration work, not the default.
visibility.risk: Medium-High — an attacker who compromises a project owner/editor can suppress read and app telemetry in that project; only orgs that proactively deployed org-level aggregated sinks + locked buckets retain an out-of-reach copy.

visibility.probe: Real-time alerting and threat detection over audit logs (SCC Event Threat Detection)
visibility.finding: Beyond raw export, Security Command Center's Event Threat Detection continuously scans the org/project Cloud Logging stream — including Admin Activity, Data Access, and System Event audit logs — matching IoCs, known TTPs, and behavioral anomalies (e.g. anomalous IAM grants, credential abuse, unusual API usage), with detection latency generally under 15 minutes. Cloud Monitoring log-based alerts and Pub/Sub sinks provide complementary near-real-time alerting paths.
visibility.evidence: https://cloud.google.com/security-command-center/docs/concepts-event-threat-detection-overview — 'Event Threat Detection monitors the Cloud Logging stream ... including Cloud Audit Logs (Admin Activity, Data Access, and System Event logs)' and detection latency 'generally less than 15 minutes.'
visibility.gap: Event Threat Detection is a paid SCC Premium/Enterprise feature, not part of the free/default tier — so a default project has no managed real-time threat detection over its audit logs. Its Data Access-based detections are only as good as whether Data Access logging was enabled in the first place (default off).
visibility.risk: Medium — capable near-real-time detection exists but is gated behind a paid tier and is blind to read-based activity unless Data Access logs were turned on.

visibility.probe: Container/serverless application log capture and retention (GKE, Cloud Run, Cloud Functions)
visibility.finding: GKE, Cloud Run, and Cloud Functions stream stdout/stderr to Cloud Logging automatically as platform logs, landing in the project _Default bucket with 30-day default retention (configurable 1–3650 days). These are distinct from audit logs and carry no AuditLog schema. Capture is on by default for these runtimes, which is good for operational visibility.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/logging/docs/buckets — '_Default ... retains the log entries ... for 30 days'; https://docs.cloud.google.com/logging/docs/api/platform-logs (platform logs from Google Cloud services).
visibility.gap: Two issues for an IP-sensitive org: (1) application logs default to only 30 days unless retention is extended or a sink exists; (2) there is no default redaction — if application code logs secrets, tokens, customer data, or proprietary content to stdout, it is captured verbatim in Cloud Logging with no built-in DLP scrubbing. Sensitive-data hygiene in logs is entirely the team's responsibility.
visibility.risk: Medium — short default retention can drop forensic app-log evidence; unredacted secret/IP leakage into logs creates a secondary exposure surface within Cloud Logging.

visibility.probe: AI/ML content logging — Vertex AI request/response (prompt/response) capture is opt-in
visibility.finding: Vertex AI generative request-response logging (Gemini and supported models) is opt-in: it is configured via set_request_response_logging_config / enable_request_response_logging with an explicit enabled flag, a sampling_rate (0–1), and a BigQuery destination. When enabled it captures full_request (the GenerateContentRequest JSON) and full_response (GenerateContentResponse JSON) — i.e., the literal prompt and model output — into a BigQuery table. By default it is not enabled, so prompts/responses are not retained, which is privacy-preserving but means no native audit trail of what was sent to or produced by models unless deliberately turned on.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/multimodal/request-response-logging — logging schema includes 'full_request' (GenerateContentRequest) and 'full_response' (GenerateContentResponse); sampling_rate 'a value of 1 logs all requests, and a value of 0.1 logs 10% of requests.'
visibility.gap: A double-edged footgun for an IP-sensitive org: (a) by default there is no record of prompt/response content, so prompt-injection, data-exfil-via-prompt, or model-output incidents have no native forensic trail; (b) when enabled, full prompts and responses — potentially containing proprietary source, secrets, or sensitive documents — are written verbatim to BigQuery, and the docs carry no explicit warning about capturing sensitive content there. Either choice (off = no visibility; on = sensitive-content sprawl) requires deliberate governance.
visibility.risk: Medium — default-off means model misuse is invisible; default-naive enablement risks proliferating proprietary prompt/response content into a BigQuery table that must then be access-controlled and retention-managed.

visibility.probe: Security Command Center tier gating — behavioral threat detection (Event/Container/VM Threat Detection), attack-path simulation, virtual red teaming, and toxic-combination scoring are Premium/Enterprise-only and opt-in; Standard (the free default) has essentially none of it
visibility.finding: SCC ships in three tiers. The default, free Standard tier is explicitly 'offered at no additional charge' and provides only posture/compliance basics. ALL behavioral threat detection is paywalled: Event Threat Detection (matches Cloud Logging streams to IoCs and adversarial techniques), Container Threat Detection (analyzes guest-kernel behavior in GKE containers), and VM Threat Detection (cryptomining/rootkit scanning of Compute Engine VMs) are each documented as 'Available for Premium and Enterprise service tiers' — i.e., absent from Standard. Attack-path simulation, attack-exposure scores, virtual red teaming, and toxic-combination/Issues scoring also require Premium or Enterprise and need an org-level activation. Managed threat hunting (Mandiant Threat Defense) and Google SecOps SIEM/SOAR are Enterprise-only and not activated by default ('contact your sales representative'). Premium is pay-as-you-go (consumption-priced against monitored resources) or subscription; Enterprise is a fixed-price subscription. So an org sitting on the default Standard tier has NO behavioral/runtime threat detection and NO attack-path analysis — the inventory's 'SCC Event Threat Detection' is real as a product but is paid and opt-in, not default-on coverage.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/security-command-center/docs/service-tiers — service-tiers comparison table; corroborated by https://docs.cloud.google.com/security-command-center/docs/overview-threats ('Event Threat Detection ... Available for Premium and Enterprise service tiers'; same wording for Container and VM Threat Detection) and https://cloud.google.com/security-command-center/docs/concepts-container-threat-detection-overview
visibility.gap: For a multi-project AI/IP org under one Organization, the single most important visibility capability — does anyone actually detect a compromised workload, an exfiltration attempt from a model-weights bucket, a cryptominer on a GPU node, or an attack path to a high-value project — is entirely behind Premium/Enterprise and entirely opt-in. The default posture is detection-blind: Standard surfaces only static misconfiguration/compliance findings, with no runtime or log-based threat signal. Premium/Enterprise threat detection and attack-path simulation also require org-level activation, so a single team enabling SCC on its own project cannot self-serve full behavioral coverage. Well-architected baseline expects threat detection to be a deliberately enabled, org-wide control; here the default tier silently provides none of it while the console still presents 'Security Command Center' as active.
visibility.risk: An org that assumes 'SCC is on' has, by default, zero behavioral threat detection and zero attack-path/toxic-combination analysis across all its projects — a compromised GKE/GPU container, a VM rootkit, an anomalous service-account token use, or a path from an internet-exposed Cloud Run service to a model-weights bucket would generate no SCC threat finding. The gap is invisible until an incident, because Standard still produces (limited) findings and looks like coverage.

visibility.probe: Standard-tier baseline erosion — as of the Feb 2026 'enhanced Standard' migration, newly activated Standard orgs no longer get Security Health Analytics at all; even the free misconfiguration-findings floor is now thinner and partly relocated to Compliance Manager
visibility.finding: Google migrated the Standard tier to a 'new set of capabilities' starting February 11, 2026, and auto-activates Standard 'in some organizations where it isn't yet activated and that don't have data residency requirements' (only if Premium isn't already active). Critically, for a newly activated Standard org (not migrated from Standard-legacy), the docs state Security Health Analytics 'isn't available' as the familiar full detector set — 'A modified set of Security Health Analytics capabilities is available,' with most detectors migrated to Compliance Manager controls in the Security Essentials framework and labeled LAUNCH_STATE_DEPRECATED. Orgs migrated from the legacy Standard tier retain only ~13 named detectors (e.g., OPEN_SSH_PORT, OPEN_RDP_PORT, PUBLIC_IP_ADDRESS, PUBLIC_SQL_INSTANCE, PUBLIC_DATASET, SSL_NOT_ENFORCED) plus a handful of unmigrated IAM/bucket detectors. New enhanced Standard adds Compliance Manager, Data Security Posture Management (DSPM), Vulnerability Assessment for Google Cloud, and Mandiant CVE assessments — but drops Sensitive Data Protection discovery and Web Security Scanner custom scans. Premium is required for 'all detectors for Google Cloud' and custom SHA modules.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/security-command-center/docs/migrate-standard-legacy ('Starting February 11, 2026, the Security Command Center Standard tier will be migrated to a new set of capabilities'; 'Security Command Center Standard will be automatically activated in some organizations...'); detector subset enumerated at https://docs.cloud.google.com/security-command-center/docs/concepts-security-health-analytics
visibility.gap: The free/default floor is now ambiguous and shifting: a brand-new org activating Standard does not get the canonical Security Health Analytics detector set everyone assumes 'SCC Standard' includes — coverage is split across Compliance Manager, DSPM, and Vulnerability Assessment, several of which must be separately enabled (docs 'recommend that you enable Vulnerability Assessment'). Familiar findings (full SHA, Sensitive Data Protection discovery, Web Security Scanner custom scans) are silently absent or relocated. Documentation describing 'Standard includes Security Health Analytics' is now outdated for new activations, making the actual free baseline easy to over-credit.
visibility.risk: A multi-project org relying on the default free tier for even basic misconfiguration detection (public buckets, open firewall ports, public SQL instances exposing IP) may find the detector it expected is deprecated, relocated to a Compliance Manager control it never enabled, or unavailable on a fresh Standard activation — a quiet downgrade of the no-cost security floor that protects proprietary code and model artifacts.

visibility.probe: SCC Security Posture service: managed posture-as-code (Org Policy + SHA detectors) with continuous drift detection across the org/folder/project hierarchy — is it default-on for a multi-project estate?
visibility.finding: GCP does ship a genuine config-state posture-management service distinct from runtime threat detection. The Security Command Center 'security posture' service lets you codify a baseline as 'policy sets' combining preventative controls (Organization Policy constraints, including custom constraints) and detective controls (Security Health Analytics detectors, including custom modules), package them into a deployable 'posture', and apply it at the organization, folder, or project level with inheritance to child resources (one posture per node; lower-level deployments override higher). It ships predefined templates (Secure by Default, Secure AI essentials/extended, BigQuery/Cloud Storage/VPC, and compliance baselines CIS 2.0, NIST 800-53, ISO 27001, PCI DSS). Continuous DRIFT detection is real and the differentiator: a change made outside the posture (e.g., an admin editing a constraint directly in the console rather than via the posture deployment) raises an SCC finding, and 'A finding related to your change should appear within five minutes.' BUT two qualifiers make 'default-on' an overstatement: (1) It is PAID-TIER GATED — 'The security posture service is only available to you if you purchase a subscription of the Security Command Center Premium tier or the Enterprise tier.' Standard tier does not get it. (2) Even on a paid tier it is only AUTO-ENABLED, not auto-configured: the service 'is automatically enabled when you activate Security Command Center at the organization level', but to obtain any value you must manually 'Create a posture YAML file... Create a posture in Google Cloud... Deploy the posture' under roles/securityposture.admin. With zero deployed postures there is nothing to detect drift against. SCC itself is also not active by default — it must be explicitly activated/purchased at the org or project level.
visibility.evidence: https://cloud.google.com/security-command-center/docs/security-posture-overview ("The security posture service is only available to you if you purchase a subscription of the Security Command Center Premium tier or the Enterprise tier."; "The security posture service is automatically enabled when you activate Security Command Center at the organization level."; preventative=Org Policy constraints, detective=SHA detectors; deploy at organization/folder/project). Drift latency: https://cloud.google.com/security-command-center/docs/how-to-use-security-posture ("A finding related to your change should appear within five minutes."). Tier gating corroborated: https://cloud.google.com/security-command-center/docs/service-tiers and https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/new-custom-security-posture-controls-and-threat-detections-in-security-command-center (custom posture modules tied to SCC Premium).
visibility.gap: For a multi-team org with dozens of projects under one Organization holding proprietary IP and model weights, the guardrail-drift detector that keeps projects from sliding off baseline is (a) entirely absent unless someone has bought SCC Premium or Enterprise org-wide — the free Standard tier, which is what an org gets by default, has no posture service at all; and (b) inert until a security team authors and deploys posture YAML. There is no out-of-the-box, pre-deployed baseline posture that ships covering the whole hierarchy; 'auto-enabled' only means the API is on, not that drift is being watched. The one-posture-per-node model also means org/folder/project baselines are coarse-grained (a single posture per project) rather than freely composable. Net: the mechanism a many-project estate relies on to prevent baseline drift is opt-in, paid, and manual-to-operationalize — the opposite of default-on.
visibility.risk: Without a purchased SCC Premium/Enterprise subscription and explicitly authored+deployed postures, individual project teams can silently relax Org Policy constraints (e.g., disable public-access-prevention on a Cloud Storage bucket holding source code, weaken a firewall, re-enable external IPs on a GPU VM) with no drift finding ever generated, because nothing is monitoring the config baseline. An org that assumes 'Google has a posture service' is covered will have dozens of projects drifting off baseline undetected. Conversely, an org that does buy Premium and deploys the Secure AI / Secure by Default templates gets genuine ~5-minute drift alerting across the hierarchy — a real strength, but only after deliberate, paid, ongoing investment.

visibility.probe: Sensitive Data Protection (Cloud DLP) discovery scanning to inventory where proprietary IP, training data, and PII physically live across BigQuery, Cloud Storage, Cloud SQL, and Vertex AI datasets
visibility.finding: GCP has a genuinely strong, well-architected data-discovery capability. The Sensitive Data Protection (SDP, formerly Cloud DLP) 'discovery' service generates automated data profiles across an entire Organization, folder, or project, at column/table level for BigQuery and Cloud SQL and at the bucket/file-cluster level for Cloud Storage, and can profile Vertex AI training/tuning data where it lives in BigQuery tables or Cloud Storage buckets. It can also profile Amazon S3 and Azure Blob. Profiles can be published to Security Command Center and BigQuery for analysis. The org->folder->project scoping means one config can cover hundreds of projects. CRITICAL: it is NOT default-on. The docs are explicit: 'The discovery service is not enabled by default'; 'You enable sensitive data discovery by creating a discovery scan configuration.' Org/folder-level profiling additionally requires designating a 'service agent container' project to track billed charges, and discovery is a separately billed service (per-scan or subscription pricing based on data volume and frequency). So for the target org, knowing where its crown-jewel IP sits is achievable but only after an admin explicitly configures, scopes, and pays for it; until then the inventory is blank and exfiltration controls (VPC-SC, PAP, IAM) are applied without knowing where the sensitive data actually resides.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/sensitive-data-protection/docs/data-profiles — 'You enable sensitive data discovery by creating a discovery scan configuration (also called a data profile configuration).' Supported resources and Vertex AI coverage: https://docs.cloud.google.com/sensitive-data-protection/docs/discovery-for-vertex-ai ; org/folder scoping and service-agent-container billing project: https://docs.cloud.google.com/sensitive-data-protection/docs/profile-org-folder ; SCC publishing: https://docs.cloud.google.com/sensitive-data-protection/docs/send-profiles-to-scc
visibility.gap: Versus well-architected best practice (know where your sensitive data is before designing controls), the gap is that this foundational visibility is opt-in, separately billed, and requires per-source configuration plus a billing-container project for org-wide scope — there is no default baseline inventory of where IP/PII lives. A new multi-team org with proprietary source code, model weights, and internal docs spread across hundreds of buckets/datasets gets ZERO discovery until someone deliberately stands up and funds SDP. Discovery also primarily covers structured/columnar stores plus Cloud Storage file clusters; it does not profile arbitrary blobs/source-code repos with the same granularity, and model-weight artifacts in GCS are summarized at the bucket/file-cluster level rather than inspected as IP per se. Source code in Cloud Source Repositories or Artifact Registry is not a discovery target.
visibility.risk: Without explicitly enabling and funding SDP discovery, the org operates blind to where its proprietary IP and credentials physically sit. VPC Service Controls perimeters and Principal Access Boundary policies then protect resources without anyone knowing which buckets/datasets hold the crown jewels, so a mislabeled or unperimetered bucket holding model weights or source exports can be exfiltrated without ever tripping a data-aware control. The blank-by-default posture is itself the risk: the most security-conscious teams enable it; the rushed ones never do.

visibility.probe: Automated discovery of leaked/unencrypted secrets and credentials (secrets sprawl) across the environment via Sensitive Data Protection secrets discovery
visibility.finding: SDP offers a dedicated 'secrets discovery' mode that periodically scans Cloud Run build and runtime environment variables for unencrypted secrets (passwords, auth tokens, Google Cloud credentials per the 'Credentials and secrets' infoTypes) and reports them to Security Command Center as findings. This is a real, useful credential-sprawl control. However its scope is narrow and footgun-laden: it is NOT default-on (must be configured at org or project level), it only covers Cloud Run functions and service revisions' environment variables (NOT Compute Engine instance metadata, GKE secrets/ConfigMaps, Cloud Build, source code, or general object storage), it generates a maximum of ONE finding per resource (so multiple distinct leaked secrets in one service collapse into a single finding), 'no data profiles are generated,' and findings are 'only available through Security Command Center' — which itself must be activated at the organization level (Premium/Enterprise) for the feature to work at all.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/sensitive-data-protection/docs/secrets-discovery — secrets discovery 'periodically scans Cloud Run for secrets in build and runtime environment variables'; 'any findings are only available through Security Command Center'; 'Sensitive Data Protection generates a maximum of one finding per resource'; requires SCC activated at the organization level.
visibility.gap: Against best-practice secret-sprawl detection (broad, default-on scanning of code, config, metadata, and object stores), GCP's native secrets discovery is opt-in, confined to Cloud Run env vars, deduplicated to one finding per resource (masking the true count/spread of leaked credentials), and gated behind org-level SCC activation. For an org whose threat model is IP/credential theft, credentials leaked in GCE startup scripts/metadata, GKE manifests, Cloud Build substitutions, Terraform state in GCS, or checked-in source are not covered by this feature at all. The capability's name ('secrets discovery') implies broader coverage than the Cloud-Run-env-var reality delivers.
visibility.risk: An org could enable SDP secrets discovery, see few findings, and falsely conclude its credential hygiene is good — while long-lived service-account JSON keys and API tokens sit in GCE metadata, GKE secrets, build configs, or GCS-stored Terraform state, none of which this feature inspects. The one-finding-per-resource cap further understates exposure. Effective credential-sprawl coverage requires SCC Premium/Enterprise plus complementary tooling, raising cost and configuration burden before any visibility exists.

identity_granularity.probe: Basic/primitive roles (Owner/Editor/Viewer) as the central RBAC footgun
identity_granularity.finding: Three legacy basic roles exist and are grantable everywhere: roles/owner, roles/editor, roles/viewer. Google itself states they 'include thousands of permissions across all Google Cloud services' and warns: 'In production environments, do not grant basic roles unless there is no alternative. Instead, grant the most limited predefined roles or custom roles that meet your needs.' These roles remain fully available in the console grant UI and are commonly the path of least resistance. Editor in particular is the auto-grant historically attached to default service accounts. Basic roles also cannot carry IAM Conditions, so a basic grant is unconditional by construction.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/roles-overview — 'Basic roles include thousands of permissions across all Google Cloud services. In production environments, do not grant basic roles unless there is no alternative.'
identity_granularity.gap: The footgun is ergonomic, not capability: roles/editor is one click in the IAM grant dialog and grants broad write across nearly every service in a project. For a multi-team org with proprietary IP, a single Editor grant at a folder or org node silently confers write across every project beneath it. There is no default-on guardrail blocking basic-role grants (no org policy constraint to forbid roles/owner|editor|viewer specifically); preventing it requires custom org policy / IAM deny policies the org must author itself.
identity_granularity.risk: An over-broad Editor/Owner grant on a folder gives a team write/read across other teams' projects (source, model weights, internal docs), defeating the project-as-isolation-unit model. Owner additionally allows changing IAM, enabling privilege escalation and lateral movement across the org.

identity_granularity.probe: Predefined + custom roles and the per-permission litmus test (reset a VM but not create/delete)
identity_granularity.finding: Per-permission granularity is real and writable. compute.instances.reset, compute.instances.start, compute.instances.stop, compute.instances.create, and compute.instances.delete all exist as separate, testable IAM permissions. A custom role can include reset/start/stop while omitting create/delete, satisfying the litmus test exactly. Custom roles can be defined at org or project level by selecting individual permissions; predefined roles offer granular per-service bundles (e.g. roles/compute.instanceAdmin variants). Permissions are enumerable via gcloud/API for a given project or org.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/roles-permissions/compute (lists compute.instances.reset, .start, .stop, .create, .delete as distinct permissions) and https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/creating-custom-roles (build custom roles from individual permissions at org/project level).
identity_granularity.gap: Granularity exists but the burden is on the org to use it: custom roles must be hand-authored and maintained, and custom roles defined at project level are not automatically shared across projects (org-level custom roles are needed for reuse). There is no default least-privilege role for common 'operator' tasks, so teams default to broader predefined or basic roles. Custom roles also do not auto-update when Google adds new permissions to a service.
identity_granularity.risk: Without disciplined custom-role authoring, teams over-grant to avoid the maintenance cost, eroding least privilege across a multi-team org.

identity_granularity.probe: IAM Conditions (resource/request attribute conditions) and least-privilege ergonomics
identity_granularity.finding: IAM Conditions allow CEL expressions on request.time (expiring/temporary access) and resource.name/resource.type/resource.service (scope a role to specific resources). This enables time-bound grants and resource-scoped grants without new roles. However, resource-based conditions are only supported by a subset of services/resource types: 'Only some resource types accept conditions in role bindings.' Conditions cannot be attached to basic roles, nor to allUsers/allAuthenticatedUsers grants. Cloud Storage requires uniform bucket-level access for bucket conditions.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/conditions-overview ('Only some resource types accept conditions in role bindings') and https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/resource-types-with-conditional-roles (enumerated supported resource types).
identity_granularity.gap: Resource-based conditions are NOT universal — many resource types don't accept resource.name conditions, so for those services the only scoping levers are role granularity + resource hierarchy placement. This is a 'Documented but Misleading' adjacent risk: the term 'IAM Conditions' implies you can attribute-scope any grant, but the supported-resource-type list is the real constraint and is easy to miss. Authoring CEL conditions correctly (e.g. pairing resource.name with resource.type) is also an expert task.
identity_granularity.risk: Teams assume a condition will scope a grant and write one against an unsupported resource type, where it silently fails to narrow access or is rejected — leading to either over-broad grants or a false sense of containment around proprietary data.

identity_granularity.probe: Long-lived service-account JSON keys (the #1 GCP credential footgun)
identity_granularity.finding: User-managed service account keys are downloadable JSON private keys that, per Google, 'don't have an expiry time and stay valid until you delete them.' They are bearer credentials usable from anywhere. Google explicitly classifies them as powerful, risky credentials and steers users to keyless alternatives (Workload Identity Federation, impersonation, attached service accounts). Mitigations exist: a project/folder/org-level key-expiry setting (constraints/iam.serviceAccountKeyExpiryHours), key rotation guidance, and key disable/enable. But by default a created key never expires.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/best-practices-for-managing-service-account-keys ('service account keys ... don't have an expiry time and stay valid until you delete them') and https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/migrate-from-service-account-keys.
identity_granularity.gap: The footgun is the default: a key, once created and exfiltrated (committed to a repo, baked into an image, leaked in logs), is a permanent credential to whatever the SA can do, with no built-in expiry and detection dependent on Data Access logs that are off by default. Even Google's own remediation (key expiry) carries a documented operational hazard — an expired key causes outages — so orgs hesitate to enable it. Inventory of where keys exist across many projects is not automatic.
identity_granularity.risk: A leaked SA key tied to a broadly-scoped SA grants durable, geography-independent access to proprietary source/model weights with no expiry, and across a multi-project org the blast radius depends entirely on how narrowly each SA was scoped.

identity_granularity.probe: Org-policy guardrails against SA keys (disableServiceAccountKeyCreation/Upload) and default-on posture
identity_granularity.finding: Org policy constraints iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation and iam.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload block creating/uploading SA keys org-wide. Critically, for organizations created on or after May 3, 2024 (announced March 20, 2024), a set of secure-by-default org policies is enforced automatically, including disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, disableServiceAccountKeyUpload, automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts (stops the Editor auto-grant on default SAs), allowedPolicyMemberDomains (domain-restricted IAM), storage uniformBucketLevelAccess, essentialcontacts domain restriction, and restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes (seven managed constraints in total). Overrides/exemptions are applied via Organization Policy at the org, folder, or project level — not via a per-resource tag exemption specific to the baseline.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/introducing-stronger-default-org-policies-for-our-customers (default-on constraints for new orgs, effective for new organizations only) and https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/keys-disable-enable.
identity_granularity.gap: The strong defaults apply ONLY to newly created organizations with verified domains — 'Existing organization resources are not impacted by this change.' Any org created before the cutoff still defaults to key creation ALLOWED and default-SA Editor auto-grant ON unless the org explicitly enforces the constraints. This is a Discoverable footgun: an older multi-team org silently lacks the protections a new org gets for free, and the gap is invisible unless someone audits the org policy set.
identity_granularity.risk: A pre-2024 org (common for established companies) runs without key-creation blocks and with default SAs holding Editor — exactly the conditions that produce leaked, broadly-privileged keys against proprietary IP — until someone proactively turns the guardrails on.

identity_granularity.probe: Short-lived credentials: SA impersonation, IAM Credentials API, downscoped/Credential Access Boundary tokens
identity_granularity.finding: GCP provides a full keyless short-lived credential stack. Service account impersonation lets a principal with roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator (perm iam.serviceAccounts.getAccessToken) call generateAccessToken/generateIdToken on the IAM Credentials API (iamcredentials.googleapis.com) to get tokens that default to 1-hour expiry. Credential Access Boundaries further downscope a token's IAM permissions below the SA's own, producing least-privilege OAuth tokens (client-side token exchange via STS is supported). gcloud has first-class --impersonate-service-account support.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-impersonation and https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/downscoping-short-lived-credentials (Credential Access Boundaries downscope short-lived credentials below the SA's permissions).
identity_granularity.gap: This is a genuine strength, but impersonation chains are themselves a privilege-escalation surface: holding serviceAccountTokenCreator on a powerful SA is equivalent to holding that SA's privileges, and it is easy to grant the role too broadly (e.g. at project level for all SAs). Downscoping (CAB) is powerful but library-dependent and not the default path most teams take. Token-creator grants should be audited like any high-value permission, which requires Data Access logging that is off by default.
identity_granularity.risk: Over-broad serviceAccountTokenCreator becomes a quiet lateral-movement/escalation path to high-privilege SAs across projects; the safe primitive exists but is only as strong as how narrowly token-creator is scoped.

identity_granularity.probe: Workload Identity Federation for external IdPs (GitHub Actions OIDC, AWS, Okta) and the attribute-condition footgun
identity_granularity.finding: WIF lets external workloads (GitHub Actions, AWS, Azure, Okta, any OIDC/SAML IdP) exchange their native token for a short-lived Google token and impersonate (or directly act as) a service account WITHOUT any SA key — credentials default to ~1-hour expiry with nothing to rotate. Configuration uses attribute mappings (how external claims map to Google attributes) and attribute conditions (CEL gate on which identities may authenticate). Google explicitly documents the multi-tenant footgun.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/best-practices-for-using-workload-identity-federation — for multi-tenant IdPs 'it's insufficient to let Workload Identity Federation check a token's issuer URL ... We recommend that you configure a ... attribute condition to check that the token originated from a trusted tenant or, in the case of GitHub ..., a trusted organization.'
identity_granularity.gap: The footgun is real and Discoverable: GitHub (and similar) use a single shared issuer URL across ALL tenants. A provider configured without an attribute condition restricting the org/repo will accept tokens from ANY GitHub repository on the platform, letting an arbitrary attacker's workflow impersonate the org's service account. The keyless mechanism is strong, but a single misconfigured provider is catastrophically open, and the safe config (correct CEL attribute condition + scoped workloadIdentityUser binding) is opt-in and easy to get wrong.
identity_granularity.risk: A WIF provider missing the repo/org attribute condition is internet-wide impersonation of an SA that can reach proprietary source and model weights — a worse failure mode than a leaked key because no secret needs to be stolen.

identity_granularity.probe: Workload Identity Federation for GKE (bind KSA to Google SA, no node keys)
identity_granularity.finding: WIF for GKE binds Kubernetes service accounts to Google Cloud identities so pods reach Google APIs without node-level or downloaded SA keys; the GKE metadata server exchanges a pod JWT via STS for a short-lived federated token. Two models: direct IAM binding referencing the KSA (principal://...svc.id.goog), which removes the extra Google SA and KSA annotation entirely, or impersonation via roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser on a Google SA bound to PROJECT.svc.id.goog[namespace/ksa].
identity_granularity.gap: Strong and recommended, but not universally default — WIF for GKE must be enabled on the cluster/node pools; clusters not using it fall back to the node's attached service account, where every pod on the node inherits that SA's permissions (a broad blast radius if the node SA is over-scoped). The namespace/KSA binding granularity is good but only as tight as the namespace isolation and the Google SA's own roles.
identity_granularity.risk: GKE clusters without WIF enabled give all pods the node SA's identity; if that SA is broadly scoped, a single compromised pod can reach resources across the project — undermining per-workload least privilege for GPU/TPU training jobs handling model weights.

identity_granularity.probe: API keys: not principal authentication; restriction model
identity_granularity.finding: Standard API keys associate a request with a project for billing/quota and 'do not identify a principal' — they are not authentication and convey no IAM identity. Google documents two types: standard keys (no principal) and authorization keys bound to a service account (which DO authenticate as that SA and are bearer credentials). Keys can be restricted (application restrictions: HTTP referrers, IP, app; API restrictions limiting which APIs). Best practice warns against URL query-param transmission (use x-goog-api-key header) and against committing keys.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/api-keys ('A standard API key doesn't authenticate a principal') and https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/api-keys-best-practices.
identity_granularity.gap: Footgun: an authorization API key bound to a service account is a bearer credential that authenticates as that SA — functionally another long-lived secret, easily confused with harmless standard keys. Unrestricted standard keys are still abusable for quota/billing theft against the project. Keys are commonly hardcoded and leaked; restrictions are opt-in and not enforced by default.
identity_granularity.risk: A leaked SA-bound authorization key is equivalent to a leaked SA credential against proprietary services; unrestricted keys invite quota exhaustion / cost abuse against the org's projects.

identity_granularity.probe: Brokered secrets: Secret Manager (versions, IAM-scoped, rotation) and injection into Cloud Run/GKE
identity_granularity.finding: Secret Manager provides versioned secrets, per-secret IAM (roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor), CMEK, and rotation support (schedules + Pub/Sub rotation notifications). Access is default-deny: Cloud Run/GKE workloads cannot read a secret until the service identity is granted secretAccessor. Cloud Run can inject secrets as env vars OR as a mounted volume; volume mounts fetch the latest value at read time, env vars are resolved at instance start. GKE supports the Secret Manager add-on/CSI with automatic rotation pushed to pods.
identity_granularity.gap: Good primitives, opt-in hardening: secrets injected as environment variables are resolved at start and are exposed to anything that can read the process environment or a crash/debug dump — Google's own guidance prefers volume mounts for sensitive values, but env injection is the easy default many teams pick. Rotation is configurable but Secret Manager only notifies/schedules — the actual credential rotation logic is the customer's to implement (a rotation schedule alone does not change the secret). secretAccessor granted at project level (rather than per-secret) over-shares.
identity_granularity.risk: Env-var secret injection plus a broad project-level secretAccessor means a single compromised workload can read every secret in the project; this is the brokering layer guarding DB creds and API tokens for proprietary systems.

identity_granularity.probe: Org-wide identity governance: IAM Recommender / Policy Intelligence least-privilege recommendations
identity_granularity.finding: Policy Intelligence's IAM Recommender (Active Assist) uses ML over the last 90 days of permission usage to flag excess permissions and recommend removing/replacing over-broad roles with tighter predefined roles — and will suggest creating a project-level custom role when usage doesn't map to an existing role. Recommendations can be reviewed/applied at project, folder, and org scope, and exist for buckets, BigQuery datasets, etc. Complements org policy constraints (key restrictions, domain restrictions) for org-wide governance.
identity_granularity.gap: Recommender is advisory and observational, not preventive — it surfaces excess privilege after 90 days of telemetry but does not block over-grants at grant time, and findings must be reviewed and applied by humans. It needs usage history (cold/new principals or rarely-exercised break-glass roles produce weak signal), and it does not, by itself, inventory where long-lived SA keys exist (a separate SA-key audit concern). It mitigates, but does not close, the basic-role footgun.
identity_granularity.risk: Over-broad grants persist for at least the observation window and indefinitely if recommendations go un-applied; the org carries excess-privilege debt against proprietary IP unless it operationalizes a review cadence.

identity_granularity.probe: Google Cloud MCP / Gemini CLI tooling: self-service scoping and the broad-viewer problem
identity_granularity.finding: Google ships official Google Cloud MCP servers (remote and local) plus Gemini CLI integration. Auth uses Application Default Credentials (gcloud auth application-default login) to mint OIDC tokens / impersonate a service account; impersonation requires roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator. Managed MCP servers use a dual-gate model: roles/mcp.toolUser to call the protocol (Gate 1) plus a separate data-access role like roles/logging.viewer (Gate 2). So tool access and data access are separable, and OAuth scopes constrain what the tool can do.
identity_granularity.gap: The self-service 'read-only' path inherits the basic-role problem: roles/viewer is itself a broad basic role spanning thousands of read permissions across all services, so an engineer granting themselves/an agent 'read-only' viewer for MCP exposure gets org-wide read of proprietary source, configs, and metadata — not a scoped read. The well-architected choice is a narrow predefined or custom read role per service (e.g. logging.viewer), but viewer is the obvious, broad default. ADC on a developer laptop also becomes a standing local credential file (a softer key-like footgun).
identity_granularity.risk: An agent/tool wired to roles/viewer for convenience can read across every project under the org; combined with an LLM in the loop this broadens the exposure surface for proprietary IP well beyond what the task needs.

identity_granularity.probe: Compute Engine default service account: automatic Editor primitive-role grant at project creation, and the iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts org-policy control plus its default-enforcement state for new organizations
identity_granularity.finding: When a project with Compute Engine (or App Engine) is created, a default service account (PROJECT_NUMBER-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com) is created and, historically, automatically granted the broad Editor primitive role (roles/editor) on the whole project. Google now ships a boolean org-policy constraint, iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts, which when enforced prevents the default SA from receiving any IAM role automatically at creation. Crucially this is DEFAULT-ENFORCED only for organizations created on or after May 3, 2024 under the 'secure-by-default organizations' security baseline (the docs say 'If you created your organization after May 3, 2024, this constraint is enforced by default'; the baseline bundle also includes disableServiceAccountKeyCreation/Upload, allowedPolicyMemberDomains, allowedContactDomains, restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes, and uniformBucketLevelAccess). For any organization created BEFORE that date the constraint is NOT enforced by default and the Editor grant still happens silently. Two further sharp edges: (1) the baseline is NOT retroactive -- it only attaches to newly created orgs, so a multi-team org that predates May 2024 must enforce it manually across folders/projects; and (2) enforcing the constraint does not revoke an Editor role the default SA already holds -- Google explicitly tells you to 'replace the Editor role with less permissive roles' yourself, so existing projects remain over-privileged until manually remediated.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-types -- 'Depending on your organization policy configuration, the default service account might automatically be granted the Editor role on your project. We strongly recommend that you disable the automatic role grant by enforcing the iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts organization policy constraint. If you created your organization after May 3, 2024, this constraint is enforced by default.' Baseline list and non-retroactivity: https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/secure-by-default-organizations ('Google Cloud security baseline constraints are enforced for all organizations created on or after May 3, 2024.'). Default-on announcement: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/introducing-stronger-default-org-policies-for-our-customers
identity_granularity.gap: For the multi-team org context, the control is well-architected ONLY if the Organization was created on/after May 3, 2024. An org predating that date (the common case for established companies holding proprietary IP) inherits the legacy footgun: every new project's default SA silently gets project-wide Editor, and there is no automatic remediation of already-granted Editor on existing projects. The org must (a) enforce iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts at the org node, and (b) run a separate sweep to strip pre-existing Editor grants -- neither happens by default, and the docs frame the constraint as forward-looking only.
identity_granularity.risk: Any VM or GKE node running as the default SA inherits Editor on the entire project. A single application-level compromise (RCE in a Vertex/GKE workload, SSRF reaching the metadata server) yields a token that can read/modify nearly all resources in the project -- read model weights from Cloud Storage, exfiltrate source from Cloud SQL/Secret Manager, alter firewall rules. Editor is the maximal blast-radius primitive role, so this converts a contained workload breach into a project-wide IP-exfiltration event.

identity_granularity.probe: Auto-attachment of the default service account to VMs and GKE nodes, and the access-scope layer (whether default workloads run with cloud-platform / over-broad scope unless suppressed)
identity_granularity.finding: The default SA is attached by default to any Compute Engine VM created via the gcloud CLI or Cloud Console when no service account is specified ('Attached by default to all VMs that you created by using the Google Cloud CLI or the Google Cloud console'), and GKE likewise uses the Compute Engine default service account as the node service account by default ('By default, GKE uses the Compute Engine default service account ... as the node service account'). On top of IAM there is a second, legacy authorization layer -- OAuth access scopes -- that gates which APIs the attached token may call. The gcloud/console default ('Allow default access') grants a restricted scope set (devstorage.read_only, logging.write, monitoring.write, service.management.readonly, servicecontrol, trace.append), NOT cloud-platform; however the console one-click 'Allow full access to all Cloud APIs' and many Terraform/API workflows set the cloud-platform scope, which removes the scope guardrail entirely and exposes the SA's full IAM (i.e. Editor, on a pre-May-2024 org) to the workload. Scopes are a coarse, deprecated mechanism: Google's own guidance is to stop relying on them and instead attach a least-privilege user-managed SA (or use Workload Identity Federation for GKE so pods get their own identity decoupled from the node).
identity_granularity.evidence: VM auto-attach + GKE default node SA + scope layer: https://docs.cloud.google.com/compute/docs/access/service-accounts (default SA 'Attached by default to all VMs', and 'IAM restricts access to APIs based on the IAM roles ... Access scopes potentially further limit access to API methods'). GKE default: https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/security/configure-node-service-accounts ('By default, GKE uses the Compute Engine default service account ... as the node service account' and recommends a custom least-privilege SA). Default-scope value via gcloud (devstorage.read_only baseline): https://docs.cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/compute/instances/create
identity_granularity.gap: The auto-attach default is the second half of the footgun and has no org-policy off-switch analogous to the Editor grant: there is no default-enforced constraint forcing new VMs/GKE node pools to use a least-privilege SA or to omit cloud-platform scope. compute.skipDefaultNetworkCreation has a network analogue in the baseline, but there is NO equivalent baseline constraint that suppresses default-SA attachment or caps access scopes -- it is left to per-resource discipline or third-party guardrails/CIS rules. The scope layer is also misleading as a safety net: it is widely set to cloud-platform by IaC and the console 'full access' button, and Google itself deprecates relying on scopes, so teams that 'trust the scope restriction' are often not actually restricted.
identity_granularity.risk: In a multi-team org, individual teams creating VMs/GKE clusters via Terraform or the console 'full access' path will, by default and without org-level prevention, attach the Editor-holding default SA with cloud-platform scope. The result is fleets of GPU/Vertex/GKE nodes each carrying a project-wide-Editor credential reachable from the metadata endpoint -- the canonical GCP lateral-movement and privilege-escalation primitive. Because suppression is opt-in per resource and not centrally default-enforced, a single team's omission re-introduces full blast radius regardless of org-wide intentions.

identity_granularity.probe: Org Policy constraints catalog: which high-value IAM/compute/storage hardening constraints are default-on for a fresh Organization vs must be explicitly applied
identity_granularity.finding: GCP has a deep, genuinely strong Org Policy constraint catalog (hundreds of predefined boolean/list constraints plus custom CEL constraints), and since the 'secure by default' baseline (organizations created on or after 2024-05-03) a fixed bundle of constraints is auto-enforced at org-creation time. That default baseline is identity/storage-leaning, NOT compute-hardening: it includes constraints/iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, constraints/iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload, constraints/iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts, constraints/iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains, constraints/essentialcontacts.managed.allowedContactDomains, constraints/compute.managed.restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes, and constraints/storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess. Crucially, the six constraints a rigorous reviewer expects most for a GPU/VM estate are split: iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation and iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains ARE in the default baseline, but constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess, constraints/compute.requireOsLogin, constraints/compute.requireShieldedVm, and constraints/storage.publicAccessPrevention are NOT default-enforced and must be explicitly applied. For vmExternalIpAccess the doc is explicit that absent a policy 'all VM instances are allowed to use external IPv4 addresses' (allow-all default), and these baseline defaults do not retroactively apply to organizations created before the cutoff.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/secure-by-default-organizations (security baseline constraint list + 2024-05-03 cutoff, existing orgs unaffected); blog https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/introducing-stronger-default-org-policies-for-our-customers; constraint reference https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/org-policy-constraints (list of constraints with restrictive Google-managed defaults excludes compute.vmExternalIpAccess/requireOsLogin/requireShieldedVm and storage.publicAccessPrevention; vmExternalIpAccess default = all VMs allowed external IPv4)
identity_granularity.gap: The default-on baseline stops at SA-key/domain/bucket-ACL hygiene and does not cover the highest-leverage compute exposure and host-integrity controls. For a multi-project org under one Organization holding proprietary IP, the four compute/storage constraints that most directly reduce blast radius (no public VM IPs, IAM-tied SSH via OS Login, Shielded VM attestation, public-access-prevention on buckets) are opt-in per the Org->Folder->Project hierarchy. A team can spin up an internet-exposed A3 GPU VM with a public IP and metadata SSH keys, or a publicly-readable bucket of model weights, in a project where no one applied these policies. Older orgs (pre-2024-05-03) get none of the baseline at all.
identity_granularity.risk: Exfiltration / lateral-movement exposure of proprietary IP (source, weights, internal docs): public-IP GPU/compute nodes are directly internet-reachable; absent storage.publicAccessPrevention a bucket can be made public by a project member; the controls that would prevent this are present and well-documented but require deliberate org/folder-level rollout rather than being inherited by default.

identity_granularity.probe: Compute identity/exposure footguns: OS Login vs metadata SSH keys, and external-IP-by-default, are opt-in not default-on
identity_granularity.finding: OS Login (constraints/compute.requireOsLogin) is the IAM-integrated SSH path where access is re-checked against IAM on every login attempt and is automatically revoked when IAM is removed. It is DISABLED by default; the default SSH access path is metadata-based SSH keys, where authorization is evaluated once at key-upload time and persists statically in instance/project metadata until manually removed. Requiring OS Login org-wide is an explicit opt-in (set enable-oslogin metadata or enforce the constraint at org/folder/project). Separately, constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess defaults to allow-all (every VM may receive an external IP), so internet exposure of compute is opt-out, not opt-in. Neither constraint is part of the secure-by-default baseline.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/oslogin (OS Login disabled by default; metadata SSH keys are the default path; OS Login re-checks IAM 'for every login attempt' and revokes on IAM removal, vs metadata keys authorized once at upload); https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/access/organization-policies (requireOsLogin / vmExternalIpAccess are opt-in constraints); https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/secure-by-default-organizations (baseline excludes both)
identity_granularity.gap: For a GPU/VM-heavy estate the default leaves two standard high-leverage footguns live: (1) static metadata SSH keys instead of IAM-tied, per-session-evaluated OS Login — a departing engineer's or leaked metadata key keeps host access until someone manually prunes it, with no automatic IAM-driven revocation; (2) VMs can be assigned public IPs by default, so an A3/A2 training node is internet-reachable unless a team deliberately removes the external IP or an admin enforces vmExternalIpAccess. Both are fixable with one Org Policy each, but the secure default points the wrong way for an org guarding proprietary IP.
identity_granularity.risk: Credential persistence and direct internet exposure of expensive GPU compute that may hold model weights / training data: leaked or orphaned metadata SSH keys grant durable host access decoupled from IAM, and default external IPs widen the attack surface of the compute fleet — exactly the nodes most attractive to an attacker after IP.

identity_granularity.probe: Workload Identity Federation (GitHub Actions): attribute-condition / principalSet scoping correctness — does the documentation steer away from the over-broad mapping (repository_owner-only condition, organization-level principalSet) that silently grants cross-repo SA impersonation?
identity_granularity.finding: GCP documents the core risk explicitly and correctly. The deployment-pipelines guide carries a hard 'Warning': 'GitHub, GitLab SaaS, and HCP Terraform use a single issuer URL across all organizations and some of the claims embedded in OIDC tokens might not be unique to your organization. To help protect against spoofing threats, you must use an attribute condition that restricts access to tokens issued by your GitHub organization...' The best-practices page repeats it: a single issuer URL means 'it's insufficient to let Workload Identity Federation check a token's issuer URL,' so you must add an attribute condition checking the trusted tenant/org. So the pool-entry footgun (no condition at all => any GitHub repo on earth can enter the pool) is Documented. HOWEVER, the steering stops one level too high to actually prevent the catastrophic case. The canonical attribute-condition example shown is repository_OWNER-scoped: assertion.repository_owner=='ORGANIZATION'. That admits EVERY repo in your GitHub org — including a malicious/compromised repo or an attacker-created repo under the same org — into the pool. The narrower, correct scoping (per-exact-repo via attribute.repository, and per-branch via assertion.ref / attribute.ref) appears mainly in the principalSet IAM-binding step and in third-party guides, not as the headline default. The official google-github-actions/auth README's primary worked example also conditions only on repository_owner ('assertion.repository_owner == ${GITHUB_ORG}') and pushes exact-repo scoping into a later, easy-to-skip IAM binding ('attribute.repository/${REPO}'). The result: a keyless setup that copy-pastes the documented happy path is org-wide impersonable, not repo+branch-scoped.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workload-identity-federation-with-deployment-pipelines — verbatim Warning: 'GitHub, GitLab SaaS, and HCP Terraform use a single issuer URL across all organizations ... To help protect against spoofing threats, you must use an attribute condition that restricts access to tokens issued by your GitHub organization...'; canonical condition example shown is 'assertion.repository_owner=="ORGANIZATION"' (org-level). Best-practices reinforcement: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/best-practices-for-using-workload-identity-federation . google-github-actions/auth README CAUTION: 'Always add an Attribute Condition to restrict entry into the Workload Identity Pool' (https://github.com/google-github-actions/auth).
identity_granularity.gap: The documented and tooling-default condition is repository_owner-scoped, which is org-wide trust, not the least-privilege repo+branch scoping a multi-team org holding proprietary IP needs. In a many-project Org with many teams pushing to a shared GitHub org, an org-level attribute condition plus an org-level (or absent-narrowing) principalSet binding means ANY repo's workflow — including a low-trust experimental repo, a compromised dependency's reusable workflow, or a pull_request workflow — can mint a token that impersonates the bound service account and reach whatever that SA can read (model weights, source, internal docs). The fix (attribute.repository for an exact repo, plus assertion.ref/attribute.ref for branch, plus *_id numeric variants) exists and is correct, but it is not the path of least resistance and is not surfaced as the default in the primary example. The failure mode is silent: the pipeline authenticates successfully, 'keyless' looks secure, and nothing signals that trust is org-wide rather than repo-scoped.
identity_granularity.risk: Silent, catastrophic cross-repo impersonation. A team copying the documented repository_owner condition believes it has scoped access; in reality every repo in the GitHub org (and anyone who can open a branch/PR in any such repo, if ref is not also pinned) can impersonate the Google service account and access the org's proprietary IP. Because there are no long-lived keys to leak, this misconfiguration produces no scary artifact and is unlikely to be caught in review.

identity_granularity.probe: WIF GitHub Actions: does the documentation warn about pull_request-from-fork token minting and steer toward restricting by GitHub event/ref so a fork's PR workflow cannot obtain the OIDC token and impersonate the SA?
identity_granularity.finding: The GCP-side WIF documentation addresses tenant spoofing (single issuer URL) and branch pinning via assertion.ref (e.g. assertion.ref=='refs/heads/main'), and warns to prefer numeric *_id fields over name fields to avoid cybersquatting/typosquatting on deleted repos/orgs. But the GCP docs do not address the GitHub-Actions-specific fork/pull_request token-minting footgun: there is no 'fork', 'pull_request', or event-type guidance, and no warning that a workflow triggered by a pull_request (including from a fork, depending on workflow trigger config) can still receive an id-token and, if the attribute condition is only owner/ref-based or the ref isn't pinned, authenticate. The mitigation lives on the GitHub side (id-token permission scope, event/ref claims) and in the google-github-actions/auth troubleshooting docs, not in Google's IAM WIF guidance. Google's docs treat ref-pinning as optional hardening rather than calling out the concrete fork-PR attack path.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workload-identity-federation-with-deployment-pipelines — page shows branch pinning ('assertion.repository_owner=="ORGANIZATION" && assertion.ref=="refs/heads/main"') and the cybersquatting 'Important' note ('Using "name" fields like repository and repository_owner increases the chances of cybersquatting and typosquatting attacks ... use the numeric *_id fields instead'), but contains no 'fork' / 'pull_request' / event-type warning. Tooling-side caution to add an attribute condition: https://github.com/google-github-actions/auth (and its docs/TROUBLESHOOTING.md).
identity_granularity.gap: No GCP doc connects the attribute condition to GitHub's pull_request/fork trigger semantics. A team can pin repository_owner (or even attribute.repository) yet leave ref unpinned, so a workflow that runs on pull_request — potentially from a fork — can still mint a token accepted by the pool. The least-privilege story (pin exact repo AND branch AND, where relevant, event) is only fully achievable by combining Google's CEL conditions with GitHub-side knowledge that Google's docs do not provide.
identity_granularity.risk: A contributor (or attacker) opening a pull request can trigger a workflow that authenticates to Google Cloud as the bound service account if the condition is not also pinned to a trusted ref/event, exposing proprietary IP the SA can reach. This is the 'keyless looks secure' trap in its most exploitable form, and the GCP docs leave the reader to discover the fork/PR dimension elsewhere.

supply_chain.probe: Container Registry (gcr.io) deprecation and shutdown — current registry state
supply_chain.finding: Container Registry (gcr.io) is fully deprecated and shut down. Per Google's timeline: writing to Container Registry became unavailable March 18, 2025; GKE lost the ability to read Container Registry-stored images June 3, 2025; and on October 14, 2025 all gcr.io URLs are served from Artifact Registry, with any images not copied to Artifact Registry becoming unavailable. gcr.io hostnames (gcr.io, us.gcr.io, eu.gcr.io, asia.gcr.io) are now backed by Artifact Registry 'gcr.io repositories', so existing pull URLs keep working without code changes. Artifact Registry is the single current registry service, supporting container images plus OS/language packages (Maven, npm, Python, etc.), per-repo IAM, CMEK, and VPC Service Controls perimeters.
supply_chain.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/artifact-registry/docs/transition/prepare-gcr-shutdown — 'Effective March 18, 2025, Container Registry is shut down and writing images to Container Registry is unavailable.' Timeline also at https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/container-registry
supply_chain.gap: The migration is a real operational hazard, not a feature gap: multi-team orgs with hardcoded gcr.io push targets, CI that writes to gcr.io, or IaC/admission policies referencing Container Registry hostnames silently broke at the March 2025 write cutoff and the October 2025 final cutover. Teams that never ran the automatic migration tool risk losing uncopied images. Per-repo IAM in Artifact Registry is stricter than the old project-wide gcr.io bucket model, so naive lift-and-shift can break pulls if repo-level roles aren't granted.
supply_chain.risk: A team's build/deploy pipeline for proprietary model images breaks at a cutover date, or images are lost; mismatched IAM between the old bucket model and per-repo Artifact Registry roles causes either outage or accidental over-broad grants during migration.

supply_chain.probe: Binary Authorization — native deploy-time image signing/attestation gate (GKE and Cloud Run)
supply_chain.finding: Binary Authorization is a genuine native admission-control gate for GKE, GKE Autopilot, Cloud Run, and Anthos. It verifies cryptographic attestations (signer's public key registered in an 'attestor') against the image digest at deploy time and can require images to originate from trusted registries. It supports two enforcement modes: 'Block and Audit Log' (real enforcement) and 'Dry Run: Audit Log Only' (logs violations but still admits, for safe rollout). It integrates with Artifact Analysis to build vulnerability-based allowlists. This is a real GCP differentiator — a first-party supply-chain admission gate, not a bolt-on.
supply_chain.evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/binary-authorization/docs/key-concepts — 'Dry run mode is an enforcement mode in a policy that allows non-conformant images to be deployed, but writes details about the deployment to the Cloud Audit Logs.' Cloud Run enabling: https://cloud.google.com/binary-authorization/docs/run/enabling-binauthz-cloud-run
supply_chain.gap: It is opt-in and ships permissive: it is not enabled on clusters/services by default, and when first enabled the default rule is 'allow all images' — which 'mimics the behavior as if Binary Authorization wasn't enabled.' Teams must actively author allowlist/attestation rules to get any protection. The breakglass annotation lets an operator bypass the policy entirely (it only writes an audit-log entry), so enforcement is only as strong as monitoring of breakglass events. Each project/cluster configures its own policy, so org-wide guarantees require pairing with an org policy constraint to require Binary Authorization.
