corrections.probe: Are Admin Activity and System Event audit logs always written, undisablable, and free; and is Policy Denied generated by default and billed?

corrections.claim: Admin Activity/System Event land in _Required: 400 days, not billed, retention not configurable, bucket cannot be deleted. Everything else lands in _Default: 30-day default, configurable 1-3650 days, billed beyond default.
corrections.probe: Is the _Required bucket fixed at 400 days (free, non-configurable) and _Default 30 days (configurable 1-3650 days, billed beyond default)?

corrections.claim: authenticationInfo captures principalEmail and principalSubject; serviceAccountDelegationInfo captures the impersonation/delegation chain; requestMetadata.callerIp and callerSuppliedUserAgent are present; callerIp is redacted to 'private' for internal service-to-service calls and to 'gce-internal-ip' for some VM callers.
corrections.probe: Does AuditLog.authenticationInfo capture principalEmail, principalSubject, and serviceAccountDelegationInfo (delegation chain), and does requestMetadata capture callerIp/callerSuppliedUserAgent with callerIp redacted to 'private' (internal service-to-service) and 'gce-internal-ip' (some VM callers)?

corrections.claim: Vertex AI generative request-response logging is opt-in (disabled by default), configured via set_request_response_logging_config / enable_request_response_logging with an enabled flag, sampling_rate 0-1, and a BigQuery destination; captures full_request (GenerateContentRequest) and full_response (GenerateContentResponse).
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Disabled by default; configured with enabled flag, samplingRate (0-1), and bigqueryDestination; the BigQuery schema includes full_request and full_response JSON fields. Note: coverage now extends beyond Gemini to partner models including Anthropic Claude (via rawPredict/streamRawPredict), with request_payload/response_payload fields for partner models -- the draft's 'Gemini and supported models' phrasing remains accurate.
corrections.probe: Is Vertex AI generative request-response (prompt/response) logging opt-in, configured via an enabled flag + sampling_rate (0-1) + BigQuery destination, capturing full request/response?

corrections.claim: Security Command Center Event Threat Detection continuously scans the org/project Cloud Logging stream including audit logs, with detection latency generally under 15 minutes.
corrections.correction: Confirmed: detection latencies are generally less than 15 minutes from when a log is written to when a finding is available. Caveat worth noting in the draft: ETD can only scan Data Access audit logs if the operator has explicitly enabled them (they are off by default), so the data-read detection coverage is gated by the same opt-in gap the draft already flags.
corrections.probe: Does SCC Event Threat Detection continuously scan the Cloud Logging stream (including Admin Activity, Data Access, System Event audit logs) with detection latency generally under 15 minutes?

corrections.claim: Google Security Operations (Chronicle) ships a default parser for the AuditLog schema; the draft refers to the product as 'Google Security Operations (Chronicle)'.
corrections.correction: Naming is current. Chronicle Security Operations was rebranded to Google Security Operations (Google SecOps) on 2024-04-25. The draft's 'Google Security Operations (Chronicle)' is acceptable, though strictly the current product name is 'Google Security Operations' / 'Google SecOps' with Chronicle as the legacy name.
corrections.probe: Is the SIEM/threat product correctly named 'Google Security Operations (Chronicle)' as of the current date?

corrections.claim: (Prompt-flagged check) The draft must not assert a stale current Google Cloud CISO.
corrections.correction: No defect in the draft: this signal's findings do not name any Google Cloud CISO, so there is no stale name to correct. For the record, Phil Venables (Google Cloud's first CISO since 2020) stepped down from the CISO role after four years and moved to a part-time strategic security advisor capacity; Google had not publicly named a successor CISO at the time of departure. Any future revision should NOT reintroduce 'Phil Venables' as the sitting Google Cloud CISO.
corrections.probe: Who is the current Google Cloud CISO -- did the draft let a stale CISO name stand?

corrections.claim: (Prompt-flagged background check; not asserted in this signal's findings.) Container Registry / gcr.io shutdown status and timing.
corrections.correction: Not a claim in this Visibility signal, but verified per the prompt: Container Registry was deprecated and shut down on a phased schedule -- writes disabled 2025-03-18, reads disabled 2025-06-03, and from 2025-10-14 gcr.io URLs are served from Artifact Registry (uncopied images become unavailable). Any draft text stating Container Registry is fully shut down as of 2026 is correct.
corrections.probe: (Adjacent supply-chain fact the prompt flagged) Is Container Registry / gcr.io actually shut down, and on what dates?

corrections.claim: Secure-by-default org policies are enforced automatically for organizations created on/after roughly mid-2024 (announced March 20, 2024).
corrections.correction: Both dates are correct but the draft is imprecise. The blog announcement was March 20, 2024 (https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/introducing-stronger-default-org-policies-for-our-customers). The actual enforcement cutoff is more specific than 'roughly mid-2024': baseline constraints are enforced for all organizations created on or after May 3, 2024 (and some orgs created Feb-April 2024 may also have them). Recommend stating 'created on/after May 3, 2024' rather than 'roughly mid-2024'.
corrections.probe: Verify the draft's claim that secure-by-default org policies apply to 'organizations created on/after roughly mid-2024 (announced March 20, 2024)' — check both the announcement date and the actual enforcement date/cutoff.

corrections.claim: The secure-by-default baseline includes disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, disableServiceAccountKeyUpload, automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts (stops Editor auto-grant on default SAs), allowedPolicyMemberDomains, storage uniformBucketLevelAccess, and essentialcontacts domain restriction.
corrections.correction: All six listed constraints are confirmed present in the current baseline. Note the baseline now also includes a seventh constraint the draft omits — restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes (protocol forwarding restricted to internal IPs). Draft uses 'including' so this is not an error, but the list is non-exhaustive.
corrections.probe: Verify the draft's enumerated list of secure-by-default baseline constraints (disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, disableServiceAccountKeyUpload, automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts, allowedPolicyMemberDomains, uniformBucketLevelAccess, essentialcontacts domain restriction).

corrections.claim: Exemptions from the secure-by-default baseline constraints are per-resource via tags.
corrections.correction: The secure-by-default doc describes overriding/disabling the baseline at org, folder, or project level (e.g., setting a different org policy or deleting the constraint), not a documented per-resource tag-based exemption mechanism specific to the baseline. Tag-conditioned org policy enforcement is a real general Organization Policy feature, but the draft's specific phrasing 'Exemptions are per-resource via tags' is not directly supported by the secure-by-default documentation. Recommend softening to 'overrides/exemptions are set via org policy at org/folder/project level (and org policy supports tag-conditioned enforcement generally).'
corrections.probe: Verify the draft's claim that secure-by-default exemptions are 'per-resource via tags.'

corrections.claim: Google states basic roles 'include thousands of permissions across all Google Cloud services' and warns 'In production environments, do not grant basic roles unless there is no alternative. Instead, grant the most limited predefined roles or custom roles that meet your needs.'
corrections.probe: Verify Google's exact warning language on basic roles and the 'thousands of permissions' characterization.

corrections.claim: Resource-based IAM Conditions are only supported by a subset of services/resource types ('Only some resource types accept conditions in role bindings'); Cloud Storage requires uniform bucket-level access for bucket conditions; conditions cannot be attached to basic roles or allUsers/allAuthenticatedUsers grants.
corrections.probe: Verify the IAM Conditions claim that resource-based conditions are supported only by a subset of services, including the exact quote and the Cloud Storage uniform bucket-level access requirement.

corrections.claim: User-managed SA keys, per Google, 'don't have an expiry time and stay valid until you delete them'; by default a created key never expires.
corrections.probe: Verify that user-managed service account keys never expire by default and Google's exact wording.

corrections.claim: A project/folder/org-level key-expiry setting exists: constraints/iam.serviceAccountKeyExpiryHours.
corrections.probe: Verify the org-policy constraint name for SA key expiry hours.

corrections.claim: generateAccessToken/generateIdToken tokens default to 1-hour expiry.
corrections.correction: Confirmed: default lifetime is 1 hour (3,600s); max can be extended to 12 hours via the constraints/iam.allowServiceAccountCredentialLifetimeExtension list constraint.
corrections.probe: Verify the IAM Credentials API impersonation token default expiry of 1 hour.

corrections.claim: WIF external workload tokens default to ~1-hour expiry, and Google explicitly documents the multi-tenant footgun (need attribute conditions to restrict to trusted tenant/org for GitHub, Terraform Cloud, etc.).
corrections.probe: Verify WIF external tokens default to ~1-hour expiry and that Google documents the multi-tenant attribute-condition footgun.

corrections.claim: WIF for GKE supports a direct IAM binding referencing the KSA (principal://...svc.id.goog) which removes the extra Google SA and KSA annotation, OR impersonation via roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser on a Google SA bound to PROJECT.svc.id.goog[namespace/ksa].
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Direct resource access (principal:// member, no GSA, no KSA annotation) went GA April 2024 and is Google's recommended mode for new workloads; the impersonation model via roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser remains supported.
corrections.probe: Verify the GKE WIF direct-binding model: a principal://...svc.id.goog IAM binding that removes the extra Google SA and KSA annotation, vs impersonation via roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser.

corrections.claim: Standard API keys associate a request with a project for billing/quota and 'do not identify a principal'; they are not authentication. Authorization keys bound to a service account DO authenticate as that SA.
corrections.probe: Verify the API keys claim that standard keys do not identify a principal and are not authentication.

corrections.claim: Cloud Run can inject Secret Manager secrets as env vars OR as a mounted volume; volume mounts fetch the latest value at read time, env vars are resolved at instance start.
corrections.probe: Verify Cloud Run secret injection behavior: env vars resolved at instance start vs volume mounts fetching latest value at read time.

corrections.claim: Managed MCP servers use a dual-gate model: roles/mcp.toolUser to call the protocol (Gate 1) plus a separate data-access role like roles/logging.viewer (Gate 2).
corrections.correction: Confirmed against current Google Cloud MCP IAM docs: to use Google Cloud remote MCP servers an identity needs roles/mcp.toolUser plus the service-specific role (e.g., roles/logging.viewer). Both gates must be satisfied.
corrections.probe: Verify the managed Google Cloud MCP server dual-gate model: roles/mcp.toolUser (Gate 1) plus a separate data-access role like roles/logging.viewer (Gate 2).

corrections.claim: (Not asserted in the draft.) Implicit currency check: who is Google Cloud's CISO as of 2026?
corrections.correction: No CISO is named anywhere in this signal's draft findings, so there is no stale name to correct in the draft itself. For currency: Phil Venables (Google Cloud's first CISO, since 2020) stepped down from the CISO role in March 2025 and moved to a part-time strategic security advisor role (later joining Ballistic Ventures). As of the latest public sources, Google Cloud had not publicly named a successor CISO. If any other section of the assessment names Phil Venables as the current/sitting Google Cloud CISO, that should be corrected to 'former CISO (through March 2025).'
corrections.probe: Although the draft for this signal names no CISO, the red-team brief flags the Google Cloud CISO seat as having changed — verify current holder so no stale name is implied.

corrections.claim: Container Registry is fully shut down: writing became unavailable March 18, 2025; GKE lost the ability to read Container Registry images June 3, 2025; on October 14, 2025 all gcr.io URLs are served from Artifact Registry and uncopied images become unavailable. The gcr.io/us./eu./asia.gcr.io hostnames are backed by Artifact Registry 'gcr.io repositories' so existing pull URLs keep working.
corrections.correction: Accurate as written. Google's transition docs state writing to Container Registry became unavailable on March 18, 2025; GKE stopped being able to read Container Registry-stored images on June 3, 2025; and on October 14, 2025 all gcr.io URLs are served from Artifact Registry with any images not copied to Artifact Registry becoming unavailable. The gcr.io repositories mapping doc confirms gcr.io->us/gcr.io, us.gcr.io->us/us.gcr.io, eu.gcr.io->europe/eu.gcr.io, asia.gcr.io->asia/asia.gcr.io, so existing pull URLs keep working. Secondary source: https://docs.cloud.google.com/artifact-registry/docs/transition/gcr-repositories
corrections.probe: Is the Container Registry (gcr.io) shutdown timeline as stated (write unavailable Mar 18 2025; GKE read loss Jun 3 2025; full cutover Oct 14 2025) correct, and are the gcr.io hostnames now backed by Artifact Registry gcr.io repositories?

corrections.claim: Binary Authorization is a native deploy-time admission-control gate for GKE, GKE Autopilot, Cloud Run, and Distributed Cloud/Anthos, with 'Block and Audit Log' (enforce) and 'Dry Run: Audit Log Only' modes.
corrections.correction: Accurate. Binary Authorization for Cloud Run is generally available; Binary Authorization provides centralized deploy-time supply-chain enforcement across GKE, Cloud Run, and Google Distributed Cloud, with the two enforcement modes as described. Overview: https://docs.cloud.google.com/binary-authorization/docs/overview
corrections.probe: Is Binary Authorization a native deploy-time admission gate for GKE AND Cloud Run, and is Cloud Run support GA?

corrections.claim: Artifact Analysis provides automatic on-push scanning of images in Artifact Registry plus continuous re-evaluation as new CVE data arrives, with effective-severity scoring and fix metadata, plus on-demand scanning for CI.
corrections.correction: Accurate, with one nuance worth noting: continuous analysis keeps re-evaluating images only as long as they have been pulled within the last 30 days. On-push automatic scanning, continuous analysis, and on-demand (gcloud) scanning are all confirmed.
corrections.probe: Does Artifact Analysis provide automatic on-push scanning plus continuous re-evaluation, and on-demand scanning for CI?

corrections.claim: Node auto-upgrade is the default on new GKE clusters; on GKE Standard with COS, GKE can auto-install a default NVIDIA GPU driver matched to the GKE version.
corrections.correction: Accurate. New Standard clusters and node pools have auto-upgrade enabled by default. GKE automatically installs a default NVIDIA driver for COS GPU nodes on recent control-plane versions (e.g. all GPU nodes incl. node auto-provisioning on 1.32.2-gke.1297000+; non-NAP nodes on 1.30.1-gke.1156000+). GPU detail: https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/gpus
corrections.probe: Is node auto-upgrade the default on new GKE clusters, and can GKE auto-install a default NVIDIA GPU driver on COS Standard node pools?

corrections.claim: On Shielded VM images, Compute Engine enables vTPM and integrity monitoring by default.
corrections.correction: Accurate. vTPM and integrity monitoring are enabled by default on Shielded VM instances. (Note: Secure Boot is NOT enabled by default because unsigned drivers may be incompatible; the draft correctly does not claim Secure Boot is default-on.)
corrections.probe: On Shielded VM images, does Compute Engine enable vTPM and integrity monitoring by default (and is Secure Boot NOT default-on)?

corrections.claim: Confidential VM encrypts memory in use via AMD SEV/SEV-SNP and Intel TDX, with Confidential GKE node pools available.
corrections.correction: Accurate. AMD SEV-SNP on Confidential VM is GA; Intel TDX on C3 is GA; Confidential GKE Nodes are GA with both AMD SEV-SNP and Intel TDX (on GKE Standard and Autopilot for TDX). Confidential GKE detail: https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/confidential-gke-nodes
corrections.probe: Are Confidential VM with AMD SEV-SNP and Intel TDX, and Confidential GKE Nodes, generally available?

corrections.claim: Cloud EKM key material is 'never cached or stored within Google Cloud'; two protection levels exist: EXTERNAL (internet) and EXTERNAL_VPC (via VPC).
corrections.correction: Accurate. Google's EKM docs state externally managed keys are never cached or stored within Google Cloud and the key is never sent to Google; protection levels EXTERNAL (over the internet) and EXTERNAL_VPC (over a VPC network) are confirmed.
corrections.probe: Does Cloud EKM keep external key material outside Google ('never cached or stored within Google Cloud'), with EXTERNAL and EXTERNAL_VPC protection levels?

corrections.claim: The GKE Secret Manager add-on (CSI) supports managed auto-rotation with sync interval default ~2 minutes, minimum 1 minute, in GKE 1.32.2-gke.1059000+.
corrections.correction: The version and feature set are correct (auto-rotation supported in GKE 1.32.2-gke.1059000+, with CMEK, fine-grained IAM, and audit logging), but the rotation-interval figures are wrong. The documented MINIMUM allowed rotation interval is 120 seconds (2 minutes), not 1 minute, and there is NO documented default value -- the interval must be set explicitly (via secret-manager-rotation-interval) when enabling rotation. Restate as: 'managed auto-rotation with a configurable rotation interval (minimum 120 seconds / 2 minutes; no default -- must be set explicitly), in GKE 1.32.2-gke.1059000+.'
corrections.probe: For the GKE Secret Manager add-on managed rotation, is the minimum sync interval 1 minute and the default ~2 minutes, in GKE 1.32.2-gke.1059000+?

corrections.claim: For organizations created on or after May 3, 2024, Google enforces a secure-by-default org policy bundle including UBLA (storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess), disabling service-account-key creation and upload, and blocking Editor on default service accounts.
corrections.correction: Accurate. The baseline enforced for orgs created on/after May 3, 2024 includes constraints/storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess, iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload, and iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts (which is what blocks the automatic Editor grant to default service accounts), plus iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains, essentialcontacts.managed.allowedContactDomains, and compute.managed.restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes. Note: storage.publicAccessPrevention is NOT part of this bundle -- the draft does not claim it is, so the finding is consistent, but PAP should not be conflated with the secure-by-default set.
corrections.probe: For organizations created on/after May 3, 2024, does the secure-by-default org policy bundle include UBLA, disabling service-account-key creation/upload, and blocking automatic Editor grants to default service accounts?

corrections.claim: Marketplace products undergo technical/security/compliance review including scanning the VM image for vulnerabilities, and apps requesting restricted user data are assessed by a third-party firm against OWASP ASVS.
corrections.correction: Accurate. Apps accessing restricted user data must undergo an annual independent (Google-approved third-party lab) Cloud Application Security Assessment (CASA) built on OWASP ASVS (assessed across the ASVS categories, covering access control, architecture, threat modeling, data protection, error handling). Marketplace's general review covers technical/security/compliance and includes VM-image vulnerability scanning. Google CASA reference: https://support.google.com/cloud/answer/13465431
corrections.probe: Does Google Cloud Marketplace review include vulnerability scanning of VM images and a third-party OWASP ASVS assessment for apps requesting restricted user data?

corrections.claim: Background check on the current Google Cloud CISO seat (no CISO is named in this signal's draft findings).
corrections.correction: Not a draft correction -- no CISO is named anywhere in this supply-chain signal's findings, so nothing here is wrong. Flag for whoever owns the overall report: Phil Venables stepped down as Google Cloud CISO in March 2025 (moving to a part-time strategic security advisor role and external board/VC seats). Do NOT let a 'Phil Venables, Google Cloud CISO' statement stand as current anywhere else in the assessment. I could not confirm the name of his successor from current public sources, so the current seat-holder is uncertain and should be re-verified before publishing any CISO name.
corrections.probe: Red-team check (not in draft): is the current Google Cloud CISO still Phil Venables?

corrections.claim: Phil Venables stepped down as Google Cloud's first CISO in March 2025 to a part-time advisor role; Google did not name a single successor CISO but consolidated security-product leadership under Francis deSouza (COO and President, Security Products) and runs an Office of the CISO led by Nick Godfrey (Senior Director).
corrections.correction: Accurate. Francis deSouza joined Google in January 2025 as COO and President, Security Products, Google Cloud. Nick Godfrey is Senior Director and global head of the Office of the CISO. Venables stepped down as CISO in March 2025 (now a part-time Strategic Security Advisor / Ballistic Ventures venture partner). No single 'CISO' successor was named.
corrections.probe: Is Google Cloud's CISO leadership structure as the draft states (Venables out March 2025, deSouza as COO/President Security Products, Office of the CISO led by Nick Godfrey, no single successor CISO)?

corrections.claim: Container Registry (gcr.io) is being shut down.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Container Registry shut down for writes on March 18, 2025; GKE stopped reading Container Registry-stored images on June 3, 2025. Artifact Registry is the replacement; gcr.io URLs continue to work but are served from Artifact Registry (gcr.io domain is not dead, only the legacy Container Registry backend).
corrections.probe: Is Container Registry / gcr.io actually shut down, and on what date?

corrections.claim: Cloud Source Repositories is closed to new customers.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Effective June 17, 2024, Cloud Source Repositories is not available to new customers; orgs that hadn't used it before that date cannot enable the API. Existing customers are unaffected. Developer Connect / Secure Source Manager are the recommended replacements.
corrections.probe: Is Cloud Source Repositories really closed to new customers, and when?

corrections.claim: GTIG tracked 75-78 zero-days exploited in 2024.
corrections.correction: The official GTIG figure is 75 zero-days exploited in the wild in 2024 (down from 98 in 2023). The '78' upper bound is incorrect; use '75'. Drop the range and state 75.
corrections.probe: How many zero-days did GTIG track as exploited in the wild in 2024?

corrections.claim: Google Cloud VRP launched 18 Oct 2024 with a top reward of $101,010 and 150+ products in the top tiers; Google paid >$17M in 2025 to 700+ researchers (record, 40%+ YoY).
corrections.correction: Confirmed with a scope nuance. The Cloud VRP launched Oct 18, 2024, top reward $101,010, covering 460+ products/services with 140+ eligible for top-tier rewards and 150+ products under the top two reward tiers. 2025 payouts: $17.1M to 747 researchers, a record and ~40% YoY increase. 'Top tiers' phrasing is fine; if precise, ~140 products are in the single top tier / 150+ in the top two tiers.
corrections.probe: Did the dedicated Google Cloud VRP launch 18 Oct 2024 with a $101,010 top reward, and are the 2025 payout figures right?

corrections.claim: Access Transparency is OPT-IN, not default-on, and requires at least Standard/Enhanced/Premium paid support to enable.
corrections.correction: Partially outdated. The support-tier prerequisite is still correct: enabling Access Transparency requires Standard, Enhanced, or Premium customer support. But Google's current docs now frame it as 'a default security control for every Google Cloud organization,' so the flat 'OPT-IN, not default-on' characterization is contestable. State it as: AxT is positioned as a default security control but still requires an enablement action and a paid support tier (Standard/Enhanced/Premium).
corrections.probe: Is Access Transparency opt-in (not default-on) and does it require a paid support tier to enable?

corrections.claim: Public Access Prevention is default-enforced for new buckets.
corrections.correction: Refuted. A new bucket's publicAccessPrevention setting defaults to 'inherited,' meaning PAP applies only if a parent org policy (storage.publicAccessPrevention) enforces it. PAP is NOT enforced by default at the bucket level. Note: Google's secure-by-default org policies for orgs created in 2024+ include uniformBucketLevelAccess, disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, and the no-automatic-Editor-grant constraint, but NOT publicAccessPrevention. The default-on storage control to cite instead is Uniform Bucket-Level Access for new orgs.
corrections.probe: Is Public Access Prevention enforced by default for new Cloud Storage buckets?

corrections.claim: Data Access audit logs are off by default except BigQuery.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Data Access audit logs are disabled by default for all services except BigQuery; BigQuery Data Access logs are always on and cannot be disabled. Other services must be explicitly enabled (ADMIN_READ/DATA_READ/DATA_WRITE).
corrections.probe: Are Data Access audit logs off by default except for BigQuery?

corrections.claim: Shielded VM is default-on.
corrections.correction: Confirmed with nuance. Shielded VM is the default for Compute Engine; vTPM and integrity monitoring are enabled by default, but Secure Boot is NOT enabled by default (compatibility with unsigned drivers). If the draft asserts full Shielded VM-by-default, qualify that Secure Boot remains opt-in.
corrections.probe: Is Shielded VM default-on for Compute Engine?

corrections.claim: The Compute Engine default service account is automatically granted the Editor role (default behavior to be aware of).
corrections.correction: For organizations created after May 3, 2024, the iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts constraint is enforced by default, so the default service account is NOT automatically granted Editor. The legacy automatic-Editor-grant behavior persists only for older organizations / projects not covered by the constraint. Any claim that new GCP orgs auto-grant Editor to the default SA is stale.
corrections.probe: Is the automatic Editor grant to the Compute Engine default service account still default behavior?

corrections.claim: Binary Authorization for Cloud Run is available/GA.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Binary Authorization for Cloud Run is generally available (GA since September 28, 2021) and provided free of charge.
corrections.probe: Is Binary Authorization for Cloud Run GA?

corrections.claim: Confidential VM / TDX is GA.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Confidential VM with Intel TDX is GA on the C3 machine series (since ~September 2024), expanded across multiple regions; Confidential GKE Nodes with Intel TDX and Confidential VM/GKE with NVIDIA H100 on A3 are also GA.
corrections.probe: Is Confidential VM with Intel TDX GA?

corrections.claim: Developer Connect exists as a CSR replacement (implied current/GA).
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Developer Connect is GA, supporting GitHub, GitHub Enterprise (Cloud and self-hosted), GitLab and GitLab Enterprise, with Gemini Code Assist integration.
corrections.probe: Is Developer Connect GA?

corrections.claim: Google Security Operations / Chronicle naming (current branding).
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Chronicle Security Operations was rebranded to Google Security Operations (Google SecOps) on April 25, 2024. Use 'Google Security Operations (Google SecOps), formerly Chronicle.'
corrections.probe: Has Chronicle been renamed to Google Security Operations (Google SecOps)?

corrections.claim: Google Cloud publishes Security Bulletins with stable IDs (e.g., GCP-2026-034), CVE links, affected products (GKE, Compute Engine, Cloud Run, Apigee), plus a subscribable XML feed at cloud.google.com/feeds/google-cloud-security-bulletins.xml.
corrections.correction: Confirmed verbatim. The feed is a valid Atom/XML feed (URL now resolves under docs.cloud.google.com via 301), IDs follow GCP-YYYY-NNN, and GCP-2026-034 is a real bulletin (Apigee SSRF, May 20, 2026). Affected products include GKE, GDC, Container-Optimized OS, Apigee, etc. The exact example ID in the draft checks out.
corrections.probe: Does Google Cloud publish structured Security Bulletins with GCP-YYYY-NNN IDs and a subscribable XML feed at the cited URL?

corrections.claim: FedRAMP High expanded to 100+ additional commercial services including Vertex AI Search, Generative AI on Vertex AI, Vertex AI Vector Search, Agent Assist, Agentspace, Cloud Build, Cloud Run; nine US regions.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. 100+ additional commercial services authorized at FedRAMP High (initial wave May 2024, AI services through 2025), nine US regions, Vertex AI Search / Generative AI on Vertex AI / Vertex AI Vector Search / Agent Assist among them, many surfaced via Assured Workloads.
corrections.probe: Has Google Cloud reached FedRAMP High on 100+ additional commercial services including AI services, across nine US regions?

corrections.claim: Q1 2026 revenue $109.9B, net income $62.6B, operating cash flow $45.8B, ~$213.8B current assets, 2026 capex guidance raised to $180-190B, Google Cloud +~63% YoY, long-term debt ~$77.5B.
corrections.correction: Confirmed against the Q1 2026 8-K/10-Q. Total revenue $109.9B; net income $62.6B (incl. one-time equity gain); operating cash flow $45.79B (~$45.8B); Google Cloud revenue $20.03B vs $12.26B = +63% YoY; 2026 capex guidance raised to $180-190B; long-term debt rose to $77.5B (from $46.5B, after ~$29.5B notes issuance). The ~$213.8B current-assets figure was not directly re-verified from the primary balance sheet (SEC HTML 403'd) but the other figures all reconcile, so it is treated as plausible/uncontradicted rather than independently confirmed.
corrections.probe: Are the Alphabet Q1 2026 financial figures in the draft accurate?

corrections.claim: Access Transparency is enabled by default: 'Access Transparency is a default security control for every Google Cloud organization.'
corrections.correction: Confirmed faithful to current docs. The exact sentence 'Access Transparency is a default security control for every Google Cloud organization' appears on the current Enable page, under the 'Requirements for enabling Access Transparency' heading. Nuance worth a footnote but NOT a correction: in practice an org must have a paid support tier (Standard/Enhanced/Premium per CIS guidance), the control is validated via an Access Transparency Admin role/IAM Settings, and it cannot be self-disabled (requires a Support request) — which is why CIS GCP Benchmark v2.0+ lists 'Ensure Access Transparency is Enabled' as a (Level 2) check. The draft's quoted framing is current and accurate.
corrections.probe: Is Access Transparency genuinely 'enabled by default' / 'a default security control for every Google Cloud organization' as the draft quotes, or is it an opt-in/manual-enablement control?

corrections.claim: Five enumerated exclusion categories: (1) legally prohibited notification; (2) IAM-authorized access logged in Cloud Audit Logs; (3) access not targeting Customer Data (e.g. aggregate cross-customer queries); (4) public resource identifiers/resource names; (5) standard automated systems/system jobs.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Current exclusions page enumerates exactly these five categories, matching the draft (legally prohibited; IAM-authorized -> Cloud Audit Logs; non-Customer-Data/aggregate cross-customer queries; public resource identifier/resource name; system job / automated systems).
corrections.probe: Does Google publish an explicit Access Transparency exclusions page enumerating exactly five categories of personnel access that are NOT logged, matching the draft's list?

corrections.claim: Vertex AI Limitations section excludes: custom containers serving online/batch inference from custom-trained models, Vertex Explainable AI with custom containers, AutoML forecasting resources, Vertex AI Vector Search 2.0, RAG Engine, and Vizier.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. The Limitations section currently lists 'Using custom containers to serve online inference or batch inference requests from custom-trained models', Vertex Explainable AI with custom containers, AutoML forecasting resources, Vertex AI Vizier, Vertex AI RAG Engine, and Vertex AI Vector Search 2.0 (plus Generative AI online inference for Vision). Draft accurate.
corrections.probe: Are the draft's Vertex AI Access Transparency exclusions (custom-container inference, Explainable AI with custom containers, AutoML forecasting, Vector Search, RAG Engine, Vizier) still the documented blind spots?

corrections.claim: Access Approval is opt-in (depends on Access Transparency); requests 'may be automatically approved without the customer's action to address time-sensitive outages', logged in an 'auto approved' state; auto-approval disabled for Sovereign Controls; and enabling it increases Customer Care response time.
corrections.correction: Confirmed verbatim. Current overview states you must enable Access Transparency before Access Approval; auto-approval for time-sensitive outages logged in an 'auto approved' state; 'Auto-approval is automatically disabled for all workloads deployed with Assured Workloads Sovereign Controls or Sovereign Controls by Partners'; and warns 'The support response time increases by the duration that Customer Care spends waiting for your approval.' Draft accurate.
corrections.probe: Is Access Approval still opt-in with the documented auto-approval carve-out, Sovereign Controls disable, and Customer Care response-time warning?

corrections.claim: 'In production environments, do not grant basic roles unless there is no alternative' and 'Basic roles include thousands of permissions across all Google Cloud services.'
corrections.correction: Confirmed verbatim on the current Roles and permissions page. Draft accurate.
corrections.probe: Does the IAM Roles overview still warn against basic roles with the draft's exact wording?

corrections.claim: If a condition references an attribute unavailable for a resource, that part is 'never interpreted as granting access'; Cloud Storage API attributes in a conditional binding cause Cloud Storage methods to 'work incorrectly and fail unexpectedly'; and Cloud Storage conditions require uniform bucket-level access.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. The attribute-reference page states the unavailable-attribute portion 'is never interpreted as granting access' and that certain Cloud Storage API attributes in conditional bindings cause Cloud Storage methods to 'work incorrectly and fail unexpectedly.' Cloud Storage docs separately confirm uniform bucket-level access must be enabled to use conditions on a bucket. Draft accurate.
corrections.probe: Do the IAM Conditions caveats (silent never-grant, Cloud Storage uniform bucket-level access prerequisite, Cloud Storage methods fail unexpectedly) still hold in current docs?

corrections.claim: Errors name the missing permission (e.g. 'EMAIL_ADDRESS does not have storage.buckets.list access...'), and for existence-leaking cases read 'Permission "storage.buckets.list" denied on resource (or it may not exist)'.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. The dedicated Permission error messages page documents both the permission-naming format and the 'or it may not exist' non-leaky variant. Draft accurate.
corrections.probe: Is the permission-denied error behavior (names the missing permission; uses 'or it may not exist' to avoid leaking resource existence) still documented?

corrections.claim: BeyondProd describes how Google secures its own internal microservices (not a customer-deployable control), is dated May 2024 with a status-quo disclaimer, and 'What's next' points customers to SLSA.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Current page states 'This document describes how Google implements security in our infrastructure using a cloud-native architecture called BeyondProd', carries 'This content was last updated in May 2024, and represents the status quo as of the time it was written', and the 'What's next' section directs readers to SLSA. Draft accurate (note: BeyondProd originated as a 2019 whitepaper; the live doc's May 2024 revision date is what the draft cites, which is correct).
corrections.probe: Is the BeyondProd whitepaper still scoped to Google's OWN internal infrastructure, dated as the draft claims, and pointing customers to SLSA in 'What's next'?

corrections.claim: Google augments shared responsibility with 'shared fate', arguing the bare shared-responsibility model 'stops short of helping cloud customers achieve better security outcomes.'
corrections.correction: Confirmed. The Architecture Framework's 'Shared responsibilities and shared fate on Google Cloud' page presents the shared fate framing and the critique that the shared responsibility model stops short of better security outcomes. Draft accurate.
corrections.probe: Is the 'shared fate' framing and the 'stops short...' critique of the bare shared-responsibility model still in the Architecture Framework?

corrections.claim: Release notes are published per-product (including Google Security Operations) plus a unified, BigQuery-queryable, RSS-subscribable feed.
corrections.correction: Confirmed and current. Chronicle Security Operations was rebranded to 'Google Security Operations' (Google SecOps) on 2024-04-25, so the draft's name is current, not stale. The unified Google Cloud release notes are filterable in console, programmatically accessible in BigQuery, and offered as RSS feeds. Draft accurate.
corrections.probe: Is 'Google Security Operations' the current product name the draft uses for the SIEM (vs stale 'Chronicle'), and is the unified BigQuery-queryable / RSS release-notes feed real?

corrections.claim: (Context check) Cloud Source Repositories is closed to new customers.
corrections.correction: Confirmed for context: effective June 17, 2024, Cloud Source Repositories is not available to new customers; orgs that hadn't used it before that date cannot enable the API (Secure Source Manager is the recommended alternative). Note: this Transparency draft does not itself make a CSR claim, so this is corroborating context, not a correction to the draft.
corrections.probe: Is Cloud Source Repositories really closed to new customers, and as of when (relevant to the source-control/transparency posture context the draft sits in)?

corrections.claim: (Seat-change check) The draft references a current Google Cloud CISO by name.
corrections.correction: No correction needed for the draft: it names no CISO, so there is no stale name to fix. For situational awareness only: Phil Venables, Google Cloud's first CISO, stepped down from the CISO role in March 2025 (now a part-time strategic security advisor / joined Ballistic Ventures); as of this check Google had not publicly named a successor CISO. If any future revision of this signal adds a CISO name, verify it against this.
corrections.probe: Does the draft let a stale Google Cloud CISO name stand (the seat changed)?

corrections.claim: VPC Service Controls coverage is broad and GA for Cloud Storage, BigQuery (plus Storage/Reservation/Connection APIs), Vertex AI, Artifact Registry, Compute Engine (including restricting persistent-disk snapshots and custom images), and Cloud Run; included at no additional charge.
corrections.probe: Are Cloud Storage, BigQuery (+Storage/Reservation/Connection APIs), Vertex AI, Artifact Registry, Compute Engine, and Cloud Run all GA-supported and protectable by a VPC-SC service perimeter, including restricting persistent-disk snapshots and custom images?

corrections.claim: VPC-SC is OPT-IN and default-OFF; a new Google Cloud Organization has no access policy and no perimeter until an org admin explicitly creates an access policy and service perimeter.
corrections.probe: Is VPC-SC opt-in / default-off, with no access policy or perimeter on a brand-new organization until an admin creates one?

corrections.claim: VPC-SC dry-run mode logs but does not block violating requests; dry-run violations are auditable via the metadata.dryRun=True field in audit logs; documented workflow is dry-run, analyze, then enforce.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Audit-log filter is protoPayload.metadata.dryRun="true".
corrections.probe: Does VPC-SC support a dry-run mode whose violations are auditable via metadata.dryRun=True in audit logs, with the documented workflow being dry-run -> analyze denials -> enforce?

corrections.claim: Any single request touching resources in two different perimeters fails with RESOURCES_NOT_IN_SAME_SERVICE_PERIMETER, requiring carefully paired egress policy on one side and ingress policy on the other.
corrections.probe: Does a request spanning two perimeters fail with RESOURCES_NOT_IN_SAME_SERVICE_PERIMETER, requiring paired egress + ingress rules?

corrections.claim: Default network has four named rules at priority 65534: default-allow-internal (TCP/UDP all + ICMP from 10.128.0.0/9), default-allow-ssh (TCP22 from 0.0.0.0/0), default-allow-rdp (TCP3389 from 0.0.0.0/0), default-allow-icmp (ICMP from 0.0.0.0/0); implied deny ingress and implied allow egress.
corrections.probe: Does the auto-created 'default' VPC ship with four named firewall rules at priority 65534 (default-allow-internal from 10.128.0.0/9 TCP/UDP all + ICMP; default-allow-ssh TCP22 from 0.0.0.0/0; default-allow-rdp TCP3389 from 0.0.0.0/0; default-allow-icmp from 0.0.0.0/0), plus implied deny ingress / allow egress?

corrections.claim: compute.skipDefaultNetworkCreation exists to stop new projects getting a default network, and it is NOT part of the secure-by-default org baseline, so the default-network footgun remains opt-in to fix even on brand-new orgs.
corrections.probe: Is compute.skipDefaultNetworkCreation a real org policy constraint, AND is it excluded from the secure-by-default baseline auto-enforced for orgs created on/after May 3, 2024?

corrections.claim: The secure-by-default baseline enables seven strong constraints: disable SA key creation, disable SA key upload, no automatic Editor on default SAs, domain-restricted IAM, uniform bucket-level access, restrict protocol forwarding.
corrections.correction: The count 'seven' is right but the draft's enumeration is incomplete and uses stale constraint names. The current baseline (orgs created on/after May 3, 2024) is seven managed constraints: iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload, iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts, iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains (domain-restricted sharing), essentialcontacts.managed.allowedContactDomains (NOT listed in draft), compute.managed.restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes, and storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess. Note the IAM SA-key and protocol-forwarding constraints now carry the '.managed.' prefix, and the seventh item the draft omits is the essential-contacts domain restriction. The draft's core point (skipDefaultNetworkCreation excluded) is correct.
corrections.probe: Does the secure-by-default baseline (orgs created on/after May 3, 2024) enable exactly 'seven strong constraints' covering disable SA key creation/upload, no automatic Editor on default SAs, domain-restricted IAM, uniform bucket-level access, and restrict protocol forwarding?

corrections.claim: VPC Flow Logs are DISABLED by default and must be enabled per subnet, or mandated org-wide via compute.requireVpcFlowLogs.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Exact constraint is constraints/compute.requireVpcFlowLogs; flow logs are off when a subnet is created.
corrections.probe: Are VPC Flow Logs disabled by default and enabled per subnet, with org-wide enforcement available via compute.requireVpcFlowLogs?

corrections.claim: Secure Web Proxy (SWP) policy defaults to deny-all outbound (good posture, but SWP is a separate opt-in product).
corrections.probe: Does Secure Web Proxy default to deny-all outbound, requiring explicit allow rules?

corrections.claim: Firewall Insights now analyzes hierarchical, global, and VPC-level rules together for overlap/shadowing recommendations; lower-level rules cannot override a higher-level hierarchical rule.
corrections.probe: Does Firewall Insights now analyze hierarchical, global, and VPC-level firewall rules together for overlap/shadowing?

corrections.claim: IAP TCP forwarding requires only an ingress firewall rule allowing IAP's fixed range 35.235.240.0/20 (and IPv6 2600:2d00:1:7::/64), authorized by roles/iap.tunnelResourceAccessor / iap.tunnelInstances.accessViaIAP, and works for VMs with NO public IP.
corrections.probe: Does IAP TCP forwarding require an ingress firewall rule allowing 35.235.240.0/20 (and IPv6 2600:2d00:1:7::/64), gated by roles/iap.tunnelResourceAccessor / iap.tunnelInstances.accessViaIAP, and work for VMs with no public IP?

corrections.claim: Private Service Connect exposes a producer service as a single consumer-side IP with unidirectional, service-scoped access (not full VPC peering).
corrections.correction: Confirmed. PSC traffic is unidirectional and reaches only the service IP, not an entire peered VPC; uses NAT so no IP coordination between consumer/producer.
corrections.probe: Is Private Service Connect the PrivateLink equivalent exposing a producer service as a single consumer-side IP with unidirectional, service-scoped access (not full VPC peering)?

corrections.claim: The first finding states: 'The invoker check (constraints/run.managed.requireInvokerIam) is enforced by default.' This equates the per-service invoker IAM check with the org-policy constraint of that name.
corrections.correction: This conflates two different things. The per-service Cloud Run Invoker IAM check IS enabled by default ('Cloud Run enforces this check by default'). But constraints/run.managed.requireInvokerIam is a separate organization-policy managed constraint that admins can OPT IN to in order to prevent the invoker check from being disabled — and the docs explicitly say 'This constraint is not enforced by default.' So the parenthetical equation is wrong: the default-on behavior is the per-service IAM check, not the org-policy constraint. The constraint is opt-in.
corrections.probe: Does the org policy constraint constraints/run.managed.requireInvokerIam get 'enforced by default' as the first finding states, or is the per-service Invoker IAM check the thing that is on by default?

corrections.claim: Cloud Run services are deployed privately by default and reject requests lacking credentials; the invoker IAM check is enforced by default.
corrections.probe: Are Cloud Run services private by default — rejecting unauthenticated requests and requiring roles/run.invoker?

corrections.claim: Cloud Run services are 'only callable by Project Owners, Project Editors, Cloud Run Admins and Cloud Run Invokers' by default.
corrections.correction: The current managing-access page states 'By default, only project owners and editors can create, update, delete, or invoke Cloud Run resources' and that roles/run.invoker (and admin roles) grant invoke. The exact quoted four-role string was not located verbatim on the current page, but the substance (private-by-default; invoke gated on owner/editor/admin/invoker IAM roles) is accurate. The claim is directionally correct; treat the verbatim quote as possibly paraphrased rather than a current literal doc string.
corrections.probe: Does the managing-access doc actually name 'Project Owners, Project Editors, Cloud Run Admins and Cloud Run Invokers' as the default callers, as the finding quotes?

corrections.claim: Cloud Functions v2 (gen2) requires authentication by default and requires explicit opt-in for unauthenticated.
corrections.probe: Do Cloud Functions v2 (gen2, on Cloud Run) require authentication by default, with explicit opt-in for unauthenticated?

corrections.claim: --allow-unauthenticated at deploy, or binding allUsers to roles/run.invoker, makes a service public; the toggle needs only run.services.setIamPolicy (held by Owner and Cloud Run Admin).
corrections.probe: Is public exposure one toggle/binding away via --allow-unauthenticated or binding allUsers to roles/run.invoker, gated only on run.services.setIamPolicy?

corrections.claim: DRS blocks binding allUsers in IAM policies including Cloud Run invoker bindings; Google added constraints/run.managed.requireInvokerIam so public services can be created intentionally under DRS by disabling the per-service invoker check rather than weakening DRS.
corrections.correction: Substance confirmed: under DRS the allUsers binding fails, and the recommended way to make a public service (DRS-compatible) is to disable the Invoker IAM check rather than bind allUsers. Note the finding describes requireInvokerIam's purpose slightly loosely — the constraint's role is to RESTRICT the ability to disable the invoker check (a guardrail), and disabling the check is what enables DRS-compatible public services; the two are complementary controls, which the finding captures correctly in effect.
corrections.probe: Does Domain Restricted Sharing (iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains) block binding allUsers/allAuthenticatedUsers, and does constraints/run.managed.requireInvokerIam let you create intentional public services under DRS by disabling the per-service check?

corrections.claim: Default ingress is 'all'; the run.app URL bypasses the LB/Cloud Armor; restricting to internal-and-cloud-load-balancing is opt-in and enforceable org-wide; Cloud Armor attaches only to LB backend services and does not protect the default run.app URL.
corrections.correction: All confirmed. Minor currency note: ingress is now also enforceable via Org Policy custom constraints and Cloud Run supports disabling the default run.app URL entirely; the finding's reference to a 'run.allowedIngress' org policy is reasonable but current docs emphasize custom constraints / disabling the run.app URL as the mechanisms. Core security claim (run.app bypasses Cloud Armor, which only protects LB backends) is correct.
corrections.probe: Is Cloud Run default ingress 'all', allowing the run.app URL to bypass the external LB / Cloud Armor, with internal-and-cloud-load-balancing being opt-in and enforceable via org policy?

corrections.claim: Edge/service auth uses Google-signed OIDC ID tokens carrying an aud claim bound to the target service URL (or custom audience), fetched from metadata server, short-lived, limiting replay across services.
corrections.probe: Do service-to-service ID tokens use Google-signed OIDC JWTs with an audience claim bound to the target URL/custom audience, fetched from the metadata server, short-lived, to limit replay?

corrections.claim: Always-on L3/L4 DDoS with no opt-in and no latency impact; L7/OWASP CRS WAF rules NOT enabled by default and require explicit configuration; Adaptive Protection, Advanced Network DDoS, and Threat Intelligence require paid Cloud Armor Enterprise / Managed Protection Plus.
corrections.correction: All confirmed. The paid tier is now branded 'Cloud Armor Enterprise' (formerly 'Managed Protection Plus'); the finding's dual naming 'Cloud Armor Enterprise / Managed Protection Plus' is acceptable, but the current primary name is Cloud Armor Enterprise.
corrections.probe: Is L3/L4 DDoS always-on/no-opt-in at the Google edge while L7 WAF (OWASP CRS) requires a user-configured security policy and is not enabled by default; and do Adaptive Protection / Advanced Network DDoS / Threat Intelligence require the paid Cloud Armor Enterprise tier?

corrections.claim: Cloud Armor has 'throttle' and 'rate_based_ban' rate-based rule actions; Apigee provides SpikeArrest, Quota, and AI token policies PromptTokenLimit and LLMTokenQuota that cap token consumption.
corrections.probe: Does Cloud Armor offer per-client rate-based rules named 'throttle' and 'rate_based_ban', and does Apigee provide SpikeArrest, Quota, and AI token policies (PromptTokenLimit / LLMTokenQuota)?

corrections.claim: ESP and ESPv2 don't enforce OpenAPI required-parameter and type definitions and forward incoming requests to the API regardless.
corrections.correction: Confirmed verbatim against the OpenAPI 2.0 feature limitations doc. Note: this finding is labeled '[misleading]' in the draft, but the underlying technical statement is accurate — the gateway genuinely does not perform request validation. The 'misleading' label appears to flag a presentation/expectation gap, not a factual error.
corrections.probe: Do Cloud Endpoints (ESP/ESPv2) accept OpenAPI required-parameter/type definitions but NOT enforce them, forwarding requests regardless?

corrections.claim: API Gateway 'supports only primitive types in request parameters' and rejects external $ref.
corrections.probe: Does API Gateway support only primitive types in request parameters and reject external $ref?

corrections.claim: IAP-on-Cloud-Run now protects all ingress paths 'including default run.app URLs and load balancers' in one step, closing the historical run.app bypass for IAP deployments.
corrections.correction: Confirmed and now GA: Google announced GA of IAP integration with Cloud Run, enabling IAP directly on the service in one click (no load balancer required, no added cost), protecting all ingress paths including the default run.app URL and load balancers.
corrections.probe: Does IAP-on-Cloud-Run now protect all ingress paths including default run.app URLs and load balancers in one step (one-click), and is it GA?

corrections.claim: LB logging is disabled by default for new backend services; Cloud Armor request logging rides on LB logs; sampling rate is 0.0-1.0 (default 1.0) and down-samples logs proportionally.
corrections.probe: Is Load Balancer logging (and thus Cloud Armor request logging) disabled by default for new backend services, with a configurable 0.0-1.0 sampling rate defaulting to 1.0?

corrections.claim: Effective June 17, 2024, Cloud Source Repositories isn't available to new customers; orgs that haven't previously used it can't enable the API; pre-June-2024 users may continue.
corrections.probe: Is Cloud Source Repositories really closed to new customers, and is the June 17, 2024 date and quoted text correct?

corrections.claim: Google's recommended first-party CSR replacement is Secure Source Manager, a regionally deployed, single-tenant managed source code repo, billed at a flat $1000/month per instance regardless of users.
corrections.correction: Confirmed: $1,000 per instance per month regardless of users; one instance supports hundreds of users/repos; regionally deployed, single-tenant. Note SSM is the documented alternative cited in the CSR release notes; additional usage dimensions (storage/egress) may apply per the pricing page, so the flat $1000 is the base instance charge.
corrections.probe: Is Secure Source Manager billed at a flat $1000/month per instance, single-tenant, regionally deployed, as the CSR successor?

corrections.claim: Over May-June 2024 Google changed the Cloud Build default to the Compute Engine default SA; for orgs created on/after May 3, 2024 that SA is created WITHOUT roles/editor; governed by constraints/cloudbuild.useComputeServiceAccount, constraints/cloudbuild.useBuildServiceAccount, constraints/cloudbuild.disableCreateDefaultServiceAccount.
corrections.probe: Did Cloud Build's default SA change to the Compute Engine default SA, and is that SA created WITHOUT roles/editor for orgs created on/after May 3, 2024? Are the three org policy constraint names correct?

corrections.claim: Cloud Build provenance meets SLSA level 3 (v0.1/v1.0) but is OPT-IN via options.requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED, generated ONLY for Artifact Registry artifacts, and ONLY for images declared in the build's images field (an explicit docker push gets no provenance).
corrections.probe: Does Cloud Build generate SLSA L3 provenance only opt-in (requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED), only for Artifact Registry artifacts, and only for images declared in the images field?

corrections.claim: GCP documents the keyless GitHub OIDC -> WIF -> SA impersonation pattern and warns about the shared-issuer footgun (single issuer URL across all orgs), recommending an attribute condition like assertion.repository_owner=='ORGANIZATION', and warns that name fields increase cybersquatting/typosquatting risk, recommending numeric *_id fields.
corrections.correction: Confirmed for the shared-issuer warning, repository_owner attribute condition, and the cybersquatting/numeric-_id guidance. Minor caveat: the specific 'principalSet on attribute.repository scopes to a single repo' detail is a standard WIF binding pattern but is not stated verbatim in this particular deployment-pipelines page; it is documented in general WIF docs. Not a draft error.
corrections.probe: Does the WIF deployment-pipelines doc warn about the single shared issuer URL for GitHub/GitLab SaaS/HCP Terraform and recommend assertion.repository_owner=='ORGANIZATION', plus warn about cybersquatting/typosquatting and recommend numeric *_id fields?

corrections.claim: Private pools are dedicated workers reachable via VPC Network Peering, can drop public egress with egressOption: NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS, can run inside a VPC-SC perimeter; constraints/cloudbuild.allowedWorkerPools can force builds off the default pool onto approved private pools.
corrections.probe: Are Cloud Build private pools dedicated workers connected via VPC Network Peering, able to set egressOption: NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS and run inside a VPC-SC perimeter, with constraints/cloudbuild.allowedWorkerPools forcing builds off the default pool?

corrections.claim: Admin Activity (ADMIN_WRITE) audit logs are on by default and cannot be disabled; Data Access logs (DATA_READ/DATA_WRITE/ADMIN_READ) are disabled by default and must be explicitly enabled.
corrections.probe: Are Cloud Audit Logs Data Access logs disabled by default (except BigQuery) and must be explicitly enabled, while Admin Activity logs are on by default and cannot be disabled?

corrections.claim: Modern Cloud Build uses 2nd-gen connections via Developer Connect, installing a GitHub App; the GitHub token is stored as a Secret Manager secret in your project; admin setup needs roles/developerconnect.admin plus roles/secretmanager.admin (and projectIamAdmin for console auto-grants).
corrections.probe: Does Developer Connect install a GitHub App, store the GitHub token as a Secret Manager secret in your project, and require roles/developerconnect.admin plus roles/secretmanager.admin (and projectIamAdmin for console auto-grants)?

corrections.claim: Implied/stale industry knowledge that Phil Venables is the current Google Cloud CISO.
corrections.correction: Phil Venables stepped down as Google Cloud CISO in March 2025 after ~4 years (he was Google Cloud's first CISO, since 2020). He transitioned to a part-time strategic security advisor role at Google Cloud and is now also a partner at Ballistic Ventures. As of the available mid-2026 sources, Google Cloud had not publicly confirmed a named successor in the CISO seat. The draft for this signal does NOT name a CISO, so there is no error in the draft itself; this is a verification note that any statement asserting 'Phil Venables is the current Google Cloud CISO' is now outdated and must not be used.
corrections.probe: Who is the current Google Cloud CISO? The seat reportedly changed - verify and do not let a stale name (Phil Venables) stand.

corrections.claim: As of March 20, 2024 Google enforces a secure-by-default bundle on NEW organizations.
corrections.correction: The enforcement date is May 3, 2024, not March 20, 2024. Google Cloud security baseline constraints are enforced for all organizations created on or after May 3, 2024. (March 20, 2024 was the date of the announcement blog post, not the enforcement cutoff.) The draft's own service-account finding already uses the correct May 3, 2024 date, so the two findings contradict each other; the Org Policy finding's date should be corrected to May 3, 2024.
corrections.probe: Does Google enforce the secure-by-default Org Policy bundle on NEW organizations as of March 20, 2024 (the date the draft states)?

corrections.claim: The secure-by-default bundle on new orgs comprises exactly: iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, iam.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload, iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts, iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains, storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess, and essentialcontacts.allowedContactDomains.
corrections.correction: The bundle now contains SEVEN constraints, not six. The draft omits constraints/compute.managed.restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes (restrict protocol forwarding based on IP address type). Also, two constraint names have moved to the managed namespace: the key-creation/upload constraints are now constraints/iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation and constraints/iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload, and the contacts constraint is constraints/essentialcontacts.managed.allowedContactDomains. The current full list: iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload, iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts, iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains, essentialcontacts.managed.allowedContactDomains, compute.managed.restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes, storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess.
corrections.probe: Is the draft's list of the secure-by-default baseline bundle complete and are the constraint names current?

corrections.claim: Google enforces iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts by default but ONLY for organizations created after May 3, 2024; older orgs still get Editor on default SAs unless they explicitly enforce the constraint.
corrections.probe: Is iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts enforced by default only for organizations created on/after May 3, 2024, with older orgs still getting Editor on default SAs?

corrections.claim: Default service accounts (Compute Engine PROJECT_NUMBER-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com and App Engine) were auto-granted roles/editor; and the project creator is automatically granted roles/owner on a new project.
corrections.probe: Is the Compute Engine / App Engine default service account historically auto-granted roles/editor, and is the project creator auto-granted roles/owner?

corrections.claim: Container Registry (gcr.io) is shut down / deprecated (the signal's framing of the supply-chain landscape).
corrections.correction: Confirmed and dated: Container Registry shut down effective March 18, 2025 (writes/pushes to Container Registry unavailable after that date). Note a nuance worth stating if the draft implies all gcr.io is dead: from October 14, 2025, gcr.io endpoints are served by Artifact Registry (gcr.io repositories in Artifact Registry), so gcr.io URLs hosted on Artifact Registry continue to work. Artifact Registry is the recommended successor.
corrections.probe: Is Container Registry / gcr.io actually shut down, and when?

corrections.claim: Cloud Source Repositories is closed to new customers (the signal's framing of the source-control landscape).
corrections.correction: Confirmed and dated: effective June 17, 2024, Cloud Source Repositories is not available to new customers; orgs that had not used it before that date cannot enable the API. Existing users are unaffected. Google's recommended successor for new customers is Secure Source Manager.
corrections.probe: Is Cloud Source Repositories closed to new customers, and as of when?

corrections.claim: IAM Deny policies attach to org/folder/project, are inherited downward, are evaluated BEFORE allow policies, and support exception principals and CEL conditions.
corrections.probe: Are IAM Deny policies evaluated before allow policies, attachable at org/folder/project with inheritance, and do they support exception principals and CEL conditions?

corrections.claim: PAB policies attach to principal sets (not resources) and cap the resources a principal is eligible to access regardless of roles.
corrections.correction: Functional description is correct. Time-sensitive note for accuracy (the draft does not assert a GA status, so this is informational): PAB policies reached General Availability on December 16, 2024 (Preview began June 10, 2024). The associated Policy Simulator for PAB remains a pre-GA offering, and IAM periodically ships new PAB enforcement versions (currently up to enforcement version 3) that block additional permissions, so 'a true ceiling' is version-dependent rather than absolute.
corrections.probe: Do Principal Access Boundary (PAB) policies attach to principal sets and cap accessible resources regardless of roles, and are they GA?

corrections.claim: IAM Recommender uses ML over the last 90 days of actual permission usage to flag over-provisioned roles and propose tighter replacements, and can be bulk-applied.
corrections.probe: Does IAM Recommender use up to the last 90 days of actual permission usage to flag over-provisioned roles, with bulk apply?

corrections.claim: Workforce Identity Federation lets external IdP identities (Okta, Entra ID, AD FS, any OIDC/SAML 2.0 IdP) access GCP without syncing them into Cloud Identity at all (syncless).
corrections.probe: Does Workforce Identity Federation let external IdP identities access GCP without syncing them into Cloud Identity?

corrections.claim: VPC Service Controls draws an API-level perimeter, blocks credential-based exfiltration even with valid IAM, supports ingress/egress rules and access levels, and offers a dry-run mode to observe violations before enforcing.
corrections.probe: Does VPC Service Controls offer a dry-run mode and operate at the API layer independent of IAM, with ingress/egress rules and access levels?

corrections.claim: (Implicit cross-reference to default SA permissions) Cloud Build historically used a privileged default SA.
corrections.correction: Worth noting because it interacts with this signal's default-SA findings: in May-June 2024 Google changed Cloud Build defaults. New projects no longer use the privileged Cloud Build legacy service account; constraints/cloudbuild.disableCreateDefaultServiceAccount is enforced by default in new projects, and Cloud Build now defaults to the Compute Engine default service account. For orgs created on/after May 3, 2024 that Compute Engine default SA is created WITHOUT the Editor role. A current draft should not describe the Cloud Build legacy SA as the standing default for new projects.
corrections.probe: Is the Cloud Build default service account behavior a current footgun, and what changed (default-on/opt-in)?

recommendations.title: Enable Data Access (DATA_READ) audit logs on IP-bearing services + immutable org-level log sink
recommendations.rationale: Admin Activity logs are always-on, but Data Access logs — the only record of who READ a Cloud Storage object, Secret Manager secret, or model artifact — are OFF by default for every service except BigQuery. Without them, read-based exfiltration of source/model weights leaves no trace.

audit.how_to_check: gcloud organizations get-iam-policy ORG_ID --format=json | jq '.auditConfigs' (expect DATA_READ/DATA_WRITE enabled for storage.googleapis.com, secretmanager.googleapis.com, aiplatform.googleapis.com, artifactregistry.googleapis.com); confirm an aggregated org-level Log Router sink to a locked Cloud Storage bucket and/or Google Security Operations; confirm the _Default bucket retention is extended past 30 days.
audit.pass_criterion: Data Access (DATA_READ at minimum) audit logs are enabled via IAM auditConfigs on every service holding IP, exported by an aggregated org sink to a retention-locked bucket / Chronicle, with retention >=400 days.
audit.remediation: Set auditConfigs at the org level for the IP-bearing services; create an aggregated sink (folder/org) an attacker in one project cannot disable; enable a locked log bucket (bucket lock) for tamper resistance; size ingestion cost and scope DATA_READ to sensitive services.

recommendations.title: Enforce a VPC Service Controls perimeter around the projects and services holding IP
recommendations.rationale: VPC Service Controls is GCP's marquee anti-exfiltration control: it blocks data egress to projects/identities outside the perimeter even with valid stolen credentials. It is opt-in and must be ENFORCED (not dry-run) and must cover the exact IP-bearing services.

audit.how_to_check: gcloud access-context-manager perimeters list; describe the perimeter (status, not spec, = enforced) and confirm restrictedServices includes storage.googleapis.com, bigquery.googleapis.com, aiplatform.googleapis.com, artifactregistry.googleapis.com, secretmanager.googleapis.com; review ingress/egress rules for over-broad allows.
audit.pass_criterion: An enforced (not dry-run) service perimeter encloses all projects holding IP, restricts the IP-bearing services, and has tightly-scoped ingress/egress rules; access levels gate trusted networks/identities.
audit.remediation: Stand up the perimeter in dry-run first to surface breakage, tune ingress/egress rules, then enforce; add access levels for corp networks; verify Vertex AI / Artifact Registry are in restrictedServices; pair with restricted.googleapis.com VIP + Private Google Access.

recommendations.title: Eliminate service-account JSON keys; adopt Workload Identity Federation with tight attribute conditions
recommendations.rationale: Long-lived SA JSON keys never expire and are GCP's top credential-theft vector. WIF removes them for CI/CD and external workloads — but an over-broad provider attribute condition (e.g. trusting an entire GitHub org, or pull_request-from-fork) is a confused-deputy hole.

audit.how_to_check: Check org policy: gcloud org-policies describe iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation --organization=ORG_ID (and disableServiceAccountKeyUpload) = enforced; inventory existing keys (SA key age); for each WIF provider, review attributeCondition for an exact repository/branch match (assertion.repository, assertion.ref) and that pull_request events from forks cannot mint tokens.
audit.pass_criterion: SA key creation/upload disabled org-wide; no human or CI uses a downloaded JSON key; every WIF provider pins an exact subject/attribute condition (no wildcard org trust, fork PRs excluded).
audit.remediation: Enforce the key-creation/upload constraints; migrate CI to WIF (GitHub Actions OIDC -> impersonate a least-privileged SA); tighten attributeCondition to assertion.repository==exact and guard ref/event; rotate out and delete existing keys.

recommendations.title: Stamp out basic roles and the default-SA Editor grant — including on orgs created before May 3, 2024
recommendations.rationale: Basic roles (Owner/Editor/Viewer) are project-wide and Google itself warns against them; the Compute Engine default SA historically auto-receives Editor. The secure-by-default baseline (automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts etc.) only auto-applies to orgs created on/after May 3, 2024 and is NOT retroactive — an established org inherits the legacy footgun.

audit.how_to_check: gcloud organizations get-iam-policy / project IAM for roles/owner+roles/editor on user and default-SA principals; gcloud org-policies describe iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts --organization=ORG_ID = enforced; check the *-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com binding for roles/editor.
audit.pass_criterion: No human principals hold basic roles in production projects; the default SA holds no Editor (or is unused with dedicated per-workload SAs); the automaticIamGrants and SA-key constraints are enforced across the whole hierarchy, not just new orgs.
audit.remediation: Enforce the secure-by-default constraints manually on the existing org/folders; replace default-SA Editor with least-privilege roles (enforcing the constraint does NOT revoke existing grants); move workloads to dedicated per-service SAs; use IAM Recommender to right-size.

recommendations.title: Security Command Center Premium/Enterprise org-wide for threat detection and posture
recommendations.rationale: SCC Standard is thin and eroding (the Feb 2026 'enhanced Standard' migration narrows it); the behavioral detectors that matter — Event Threat Detection, Container/VM Threat Detection, attack-path/exposure and posture-as-code — are tier-gated to Premium/Enterprise.

audit.how_to_check: gcloud scc settings services describe / confirm the SCC tier at the org level; confirm Event Threat Detection, Container Threat Detection, and Security Health Analytics are active and that findings export to your SIEM; confirm auto-coverage of new projects.
audit.pass_criterion: SCC Premium or Enterprise is enabled at the organization with Event/Container/VM Threat Detection and Security Health Analytics on, findings exported, and new projects auto-covered.
audit.remediation: Activate SCC Premium/Enterprise at the org; enable the threat-detection services; wire findings to Chronicle/SIEM and ticketing; enable Security Posture service for posture-as-code drift detection.

recommendations.title: Apply the full Org Policy guardrail baseline across the whole hierarchy
recommendations.rationale: Org Policy is GCP's rich SCP-equivalent, but NOTHING is enforced by default on orgs predating the secure-by-default cutoff, and even the new baseline omits network/exposure constraints (e.g. skipDefaultNetworkCreation, external-IP, OS Login).

audit.how_to_check: gcloud org-policies list --organization=ORG_ID; confirm enforcement of compute.skipDefaultNetworkCreation, compute.vmExternalIpAccess (deny), compute.requireOsLogin, iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains, gcp.resourceLocations, and the SA-key constraints at org/folder level.
audit.pass_criterion: A documented baseline of org policies (network suppression, deny external IP, require OS Login, domain-restricted sharing, allowed locations, SA-key restrictions) is enforced org-wide and inherited by all projects.
audit.remediation: Author the baseline as policy-as-code; roll out at the org node so all folders/projects inherit; delete pre-existing default networks; use Policy Analyzer to verify effective policy.

recommendations.title: Lock down serverless exposure: block allUsers, restrict ingress, require invoker IAM
recommendations.rationale: Cloud Run/Functions are private by default, but 'Allow unauthenticated' is a one-toggle public endpoint (allUsers), default ingress 'all' lets the run.app URL bypass the load balancer / Cloud Armor, and the org-policy invoker guardrail is opt-in.

audit.how_to_check: gcloud org-policies describe iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains (domain-restricted sharing blocks allUsers) and constraints/run.managed.requireInvokerIam = enforced; per service: gcloud run services describe SVC --format='value(spec.template.metadata.annotations)' for ingress=internal-and-cloud-load-balancing and no allUsers in the invoker policy.
audit.pass_criterion: Domain-restricted sharing and run.managed.requireInvokerIam are enforced org-wide; public services intentionally exposed sit behind an external LB with ingress restricted to internal-and-cloud-load-balancing; no unintended allUsers invoker bindings.
audit.remediation: Enforce iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains and run.managed.requireInvokerIam; set ingress to internal-and-cloud-load-balancing for services that should only be reached via the LB; audit invoker IAM for allUsers/allAuthenticatedUsers.

recommendations.title: Use CMEK in a dedicated key project with key-IAM separation for IP-bearing data
recommendations.rationale: Default Google-managed encryption gives ZERO cross-project key-IAM isolation. CMEK in a separate key project, with key IAM held by a different team than the data, creates an independent blast-radius boundary; Cloud HSM/EKM add hardware/sovereignty custody.

audit.how_to_check: Confirm IP-bearing buckets/disks/datasets use CMEK (not Google-managed): gcloud storage buckets describe / disk describe for the kmsKeyName; confirm the keys live in a dedicated key project whose Cloud KMS IAM is separated from the data project's admins; consider Cloud HSM/EKM for the most sensitive keys.
audit.pass_criterion: Crown-jewel data (model weights, source, training datasets) is CMEK-encrypted with keys in a separate key project, key IAM held by a distinct principal set, rotation scheduled; the org policy requiring CMEK is enforced on supported services.
audit.remediation: Provision a dedicated key project; create CMEK keys with separated IAM and rotation; enforce the constraints/gcp.restrictNonCmekServices / require-CMEK org policies; adopt Cloud HSM or EKM where key custody/sovereignty is required.

recommendations.title: Turn on Binary Authorization enforce-mode + Artifact Analysis for GKE and Cloud Run
recommendations.rationale: Binary Authorization is a genuine NATIVE deploy-time attestation gate (a GCP strength worth actually using) — but it is opt-in and ships in dry-run; unsigned/unattested images deploy until you enforce.

audit.how_to_check: gcloud container binauthz policy export — confirm enforcementMode=ENFORCED_BLOCK_AND_AUDIT_LOG (not dry-run), a default-deny rule with required attestors, and minimal breakglass; confirm Artifact Analysis scanning is on and feeds the attestor; confirm Cloud Run deploy-time check is enabled.
audit.pass_criterion: Binary Authorization is in enforce mode for GKE and Cloud Run with attestation required from a build/scan attestor, breakglass restricted and audited, and Artifact Analysis vulnerability scanning feeding the gate.
audit.remediation: Define the policy with required attestors; run dry-run to surface unsigned workloads, then flip to enforce; wire Cloud Build SLSA provenance + Artifact Analysis into attestation; restrict and alert on breakglass.

recommendations.title: Put Cloud Armor on every external load balancer and stop run.app direct-URL bypass
recommendations.rationale: Cloud Armor WAF/Adaptive Protection only protects traffic that flows through an external load balancer with an attached security policy; a Cloud Run default ingress 'all' run.app URL bypasses it entirely — relevant to GPU/inference cost-abuse.

audit.how_to_check: gcloud compute security-policies list and confirm each external backend service has a policy attached (WAF managed rules + rate-based rules + Adaptive Protection); confirm Cloud Run services behind the LB have ingress=internal-and-cloud-load-balancing so the run.app URL can't be hit directly.
audit.pass_criterion: Every internet-facing LB has a Cloud Armor policy (managed WAF rules, rate-based throttling sized to per-request GPU cost, Adaptive Protection); no GPU-backed service is reachable via its raw run.app URL.
audit.remediation: Attach Cloud Armor policies to all external backends; add rate-based rules and preconfigured WAF rules; enable Adaptive Protection; restrict Cloud Run ingress so traffic must traverse the LB.

recommendations.title: Enable Access Transparency and Access Approval over IP-bearing workloads
recommendations.rationale: Visibility into GOOGLE'S OWN staff access to your content is off until Access Transparency is enabled (now positioned as a default control but still needs an enablement action + a qualifying paid support tier); Access Approval adds consent. Coverage for Vertex AI / AI services has gaps worth confirming.

audit.how_to_check: Confirm Access Transparency is enabled at the org and AxT logs appear in Cloud Logging; confirm Access Approval is configured with notification/approval routing; check the AxT/Access Approval supported-services list for Vertex AI and the specific services holding IP.
audit.pass_criterion: Access Transparency logs are flowing and Access Approval is enforced for the services holding IP, with documented coverage gaps (e.g. any AI service not yet supported) accepted explicitly.
audit.remediation: Enable Access Transparency (ensure a qualifying support tier); configure Access Approval with approver routing and break-glass policy; track which IP-bearing services are not yet covered and compensate.

recommendations.title: Network egress + private access controls: Flow Logs, Cloud NAT, egress filtering, Private Google Access
recommendations.rationale: VPC Flow Logs are off by default and egress to the internet is allowed by default — a direct exfil path for proprietary IP. Private Google Access + restricted VIP keep API traffic off the public internet.

audit.how_to_check: Confirm VPC Flow Logs enabled on subnets carrying sensitive workloads; confirm egress firewall rules / Secure Web Proxy / VPC-SC egress restrict outbound; confirm Cloud NAT (no public IPs on workload VMs) and Private Google Access + restricted.googleapis.com routing.
audit.pass_criterion: Sensitive subnets have Flow Logs on, no workload VMs hold external IPs (egress via Cloud NAT), outbound is filtered (egress firewall / Secure Web Proxy / VPC-SC), and Google API traffic uses Private Google Access + the restricted VIP.
audit.remediation: Enable Flow Logs; remove external IPs and route egress through Cloud NAT; add egress firewall rules or Secure Web Proxy domain allowlisting; enable Private Google Access and restricted.googleapis.com; pair with the VPC-SC perimeter.
